solution: Use SSL Pass-through mode on load balancer for ADFS traffic when Workplace Join, Device Registration, or Client Certificate Authentication is required. SSL Bridging and SSL Termination/Offloading break these features.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check Load Balancer

symptom: SAML authentication to relying party fails due to NameID format mismatch between ADFS and the application
rootCause: The NameIdentifier claim format configured in ADFS issuance transform rules does not match the format expected by the relying party (e.g., unspecified vs emailAddress vs persistent vs transient)
solution: Run (Get-ADFSRelyingPartyTrust -Name <RP_Name>).IssuanceTransformRules to find the NameID rule. Check format URI property. Match to RP expected format. Edit via ADFS Management > RP Trusts > Edit Claims Issuance Policy. Verify with Claims X-Ray (adfshelp.microsoft.com/ClaimsXRay).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check NameID format and claims for mismatch values

symptom: User denied access to ADFS relying party by authorization policy (ADFS 2016+ AccessControlPolicyName or legacy IssuanceAuthorizationRules)
rootCause: ADFS authorization rules or Access Control Policy are blocking the user. When AccessControlPolicyName is set (ADFS 2016+), it overrides IssuanceAuthorizationRules.
solution: Check Get-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust for AccessControlPolicyName and IssuanceAuthorizationRules. Use $rp.ResultantPolicy to inspect. To debug: set AccessControlPolicyName to $null, re-read IssuanceAuthorizationRules. Use Claims X-Ray with same auth policy to get user claims and match against policy conditions.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check auth policy if user was particularly impacted

symptom: ADFS SSL/TLS connection fails or certificate errors during authentication (untrusted cert, wrong subject, revoked cert, binding mismatch)
rootCause: SSL certificate misconfiguration: not from trusted root CA, subject name does not match federation service hostname, certificate revoked, not installed on all ADFS/WAP servers, SSL binding thumbprint mismatch, or Service Communication certificate not set
solution: 1) Verify cert from trusted root CA. 2) Subject must match Get-AdfsProperties | select hostname. 3) Check not revoked. 4) Install in LocalMachine\My on all servers. 5) Verify Get-AdfsSslCertificate thumbprint, update with Set-AdfsSslCertificate. 6) Set as Service Communication cert via MMC. If using AAD Connect, use it to update SSL cert.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check certificate (SSL). Check bindings, check cert revocation

symptom: Unexpected MFA prompts or MFA not triggering as expected on ADFS relying party
rootCause: MFA claim rules misconfigured at global level (AdditionalAuthenticationRules) or RP level, or Access Control Policy (ADFS 2016+) forcing/skipping MFA based on device state, network location, or group membership
solution: Check global: Get-ADFSAdditionalAuthenticationRules. Check RP: Get-ADFSRelyingPartyTrust -TargetName <RP> | Select -ExpandProperty AdditionalAuthenticationRules. For ADFS 2016+: check AccessControlPolicyName. MFA triggers when issue contains multipleauthn claim. Review conditions (isregistereduser, insidecorporatenetwork, group).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check claim rules in RP to see if they are correctly set for MFA

symptom: ADFS returns error for relying party: RP identifier, Client ID, or redirect URI mismatch between auth request and ADFS configuration
rootCause: Mismatch between RP ID / Client ID / redirect URI in the authentication request and values configured in ADFS. Applies to OAuth, WS-Fed, and SAML protocols.
solution: OAuth: match client_id=$client.ClientId, redirect_uri prefix=$client.RedirectUri, resource=Get-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -Identifier. WS-Fed: wtrealm=$rp.Identifier, wreply=$rp.WSFedEndpoint. SAML: decode SAMLRequest, match Issuer=$rp.Identifier, Destination=ADFS passive endpoint. Capture Fiddler trace to compare actual vs configured.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check if RP ID is same as requested RP _ Checking RP Endpoints

symptom: ADFS authorization fails because device claims (isregistereduser, ismanaged, iscompliant) are missing from the token
rootCause: Authorization policy requires device context claims but device authentication was not performed or on-premises environment is not configured for device registration
solution: Use Claims X-Ray to check device claims (registrationid, displayname, identifier, ostype, osversion, ismanaged, isregistereduser, isknown, iscompliant, trusttype). If registrationid missing, device auth not performed. Follow Configure On-Premises Conditional Access using registered devices guide.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check if a particular device state is required

symptom: User denied access to ADFS relying party but the specific denying claim rule is unclear
rootCause: An issuance authorization claim rule is explicitly denying user access, or a required claim is missing from the token output
solution: Set up Claims X-Ray RP with same issuance policy and auth policy as failing RP. Have user login to get full claims output. Compare claims against issuance authorization rules to identify which rule denies access or which expected claim is missing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check if claim rule is denying the access to the resource

symptom: ADFS authentication fails because required endpoint (/adfs/ls, /adfs/oauth2) is disabled or not proxy-enabled
rootCause: The ADFS endpoint is disabled in ADFS Management, or the endpoint is not enabled for proxy access (Proxy Enabled = false), blocking external authentication through WAP
solution: Open ADFS Management > Service > Endpoints. Verify required endpoints (/adfs/ls for WS-Fed/SAML, /adfs/oauth2 for OAuth) are enabled. For external access through WAP, check Proxy Enabled column. Enable the endpoint if disabled.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check if endpoint is disabled _ Check if endpoint is enabled via proxy

symptom: Federated user authentication fails because ADFS immutableID (sourceAnchor) and UPN issuance claim rules do not match the attributes configured in Azure AD Connect synchronization settings.
rootCause: The claim rule variables for immutableID and UPN in the ADFS Relying Party Trust for Azure AD do not match the sourceAnchor and userPrincipalName attributes configured in AAD Connect. This can happen when the trust is manually managed instead of via AAD Connect.
solution: 1) Open AAD Connect > View current configuration > note sourceAnchor and userPrincipalName attributes. 2) On ADFS server, open ADFS Management > Relying Party Trusts > select Azure AD RP > Edit Claims Issuance Policy. 3) Verify immutableID and UPN claim rule variables match AAD Connect settings. 4) Consider upgrading to latest AAD Connect and managing the trust automatically.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Check immutableID and UPN issuance claim rules

symptom: ADFS token signing (TS) certificate does not match the certificate registered with the federation partner (e.g., Azure AD or third-party RP), causing token validation failures after certificate rollover.
rootCause: If AutoCertificateRollover is TRUE, ADFS auto-generates new TS certificates 30 days before expiration and promotes them 25 days before expiration. Federation partners may not have picked up the new certificate. If AutoCertificateRollover is FALSE, manual replacement was not performed in time.
solution: 1) Run Get-ADFSCertificate -CertificateType token-signing to get current primary cert (isPrimary=TRUE). 2) Run Get-ADFSProperties | FL AutoCertificateRollover to check auto-rollover status. 3) Compare ADFS primary TS cert with partner's cert. 4) If mismatch: for partners that cannot consume federation metadata, manually send the new .cer/.p7b public key; for partners that can consume metadata, ensure they refresh federation metadata to pick up the new cert.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Did TS Certificate rollover

symptom: ADFS fails to encrypt claims for a Relying Party because the RP encryption certificate is invalid or expired, causing authentication failures when EncryptClaims is enabled.
rootCause: The RP EncryptionCertificate has expired or does not meet the CertificateRevocationCheck requirements, while $rp.EncryptClaims is set to true.
solution: 1) Check $rp.EncryptClaims - if enabled, ADFS uses the RP encryption certificate to encrypt claims. 2) Verify $rp.EncryptionCertificate is valid and not expired. 3) Verify $rp.EncryptionCertificateRevocationCheck meets revocation requirements. 4) Update the encryption certificate if invalid.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - If RP encryption is enabled, check RP encryption certificate is valid

symptom: ADFS authentication fails for a Relying Party due to a signing algorithm mismatch - ADFS is configured with SHA1 but the application requires SHA256, or vice versa.
rootCause: The ADFS Relying Party Trust SignatureAlgorithm (SHA1 or SHA256) does not match the algorithm required by the target application or federation partner.
solution: 1) Run Get-ADFSRelyingPartyTrust -Name <RPName> | FL SignatureAlgorithm to check current setting. 2) Confirm the application required algorithm with customer/app owner. 3) To update: open ADFS Management Console > Relying Party Trusts > select the RP > Advanced Tab > set Secure hash algorithm to match the application requirement.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS Workflow - Is there a signing algorithm mismatch

symptom: Federated users get AADSTS50107 'The requested federation realm object does not exist' when signing into Entra ID applications. The IssuerID in the ADFS token does not match what is registered in Entra ID.
rootCause: The IssuerID claim issued by ADFS does not match the IssuerURI registered in Entra ID for the federated domain. Common causes: 1) UPN suffix displayed instead of IssuerURI, 2) Default ADFS Federation Service identifier used instead of domain-specific IssuerID, 3) Child domain suffix used instead of federated root domain, 4) Unexpected characters in IssuerID.
solution: 1) Run Get-MgDomainFederationConfiguration -DomainId contoso.com to check registered IssuerURI. 2) Review ADFS claim rules: Issue UPN, Issue accounttype, Issue IssuerID rules. 3) Verify the IssuerID rule correctly matches the domain suffix to the registered IssuerURI. 4) If using AAD Connect, re-run the federation trust setup to regenerate correct claim rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS understanding the issuerid claim issuance

symptom: Federated users get AADSTS500083 'Unable to verify token signature. No trusted realm was found with identifier' when signing into Entra ID applications.
rootCause: Entra ID cannot find a matching federation realm for the IssuerID in the ADFS token, so it cannot load the TokenSigning certificate to validate the token signature. Same underlying issue as AADSTS50107 - the IssuerID claim value does not match any registered federation configuration in Entra ID.
solution: Same resolution as AADSTS50107: 1) Verify registered IssuerURI with Get-MgDomainFederationConfiguration. 2) Review and fix ADFS IssuerID claim rules. 3) Ensure IssuerID exactly matches the registered value. 4) Check for Federated-Token-Validation-Policy if configured.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS understanding the issuerid claim issuance

symptom: ADFS federation service is unreachable - users cannot access the ADFS sign-in page because DNS does not resolve the federation service name (e.g., fs.contoso.com) to the correct server.
rootCause: DNS A record for the federation service name is not pointing to the correct ADFS server/load balancer (for internal access) or WAP server/load balancer (for external access).
solution: For internal access: 1) Ping the federation service name and verify it resolves to an ADFS server or its load balancer. 2) Check DNS server has an A record pointing to ADFS/LB. For external access with WAP: 1) Ping federation service name and verify it resolves to WAP server or its load balancer. 2) Check external DNS A record points to WAP/LB.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/ADFS and WAP/ADFS workflow - Check DNS

symptom: Blank or white screen appears when user is prompted to sign in to Azure AD / Entra ID; no content renders in the sign-in popup window
rootCause: Device-level issues such as disabled JavaScript, corrupted Internet Explorer settings, outdated display drivers, or Azure AD URLs not added to trusted sites zone
solution: Multiple solutions: 1) Reset IE settings (RunDll32.exe InetCpl.cpl,ResetIEtoDefaults) 2) Delete temporary internet files 3) Enable Active Scripting in IE Security settings 4) Re-register DLLs (regsvr32 urlmon.dll, mshtml.dll, iertutil.dll, wininet.dll, shdocvw.dll) 5) Add Entra ID URLs to trusted sites (login.microsoftonline.com, login.windows.net, etc.) 6) Update Windows and display drivers
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Blank or White screen

symptom: Outlook Mobile sign-in using QR code fails; error indicates Intune and Conditional Access policies prevent sign-in
rootCause: Intune App Protection and Conditional Access policies block the token transfer mechanism used by QR code scanning from OWA to Outlook Mobile
solution: Have the user sign in manually instead of using QR code scan. Review and adjust Intune/Conditional Access policies if QR code sign-in is desired.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication with Scan QR Code

symptom: Federated sign-in is bypassed when using QR code to bootstrap Outlook Mobile account, causing MDM control enforcement to fail at device level
rootCause: Token transfer performed by scanning QR code bypasses the federation server, preventing MDM policies from being enforced during Outlook Mobile sign-in
solution: Disable QR code sign-in feature via Exchange PowerShell to restore username/password bootstrapping that goes through the federation server
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Authentication with Scan QR Code

symptom: Users using email as alternate sign-in ID fail validation during legacy credential registration flow
rootCause: Legacy credential registration flow loads the UPN instead of the alternate email address, causing credential validation to fail
solution: Configure the user to participate in the Converged Registration flow instead of the legacy registration flow

symptom: OneDrive on iOS fails to sign in user with email alternate ID when MFA is not prompted; shows permission error after successful authentication
rootCause: OneDrive iOS app does not properly handle email alternate login ID authorization flow when MFA prompt is absent
solution: Prompt user for MFA or have the Microsoft Authenticator App installed with the user identity enrolled in the app

rootCause: Bug in portal.office.com when processing email as alternate sign-in identifier

rootCause: Alternate Login ID with email is not supported for federated domains (AD FS). Only PHS, PTA, and Managed authentication are supported
solution: Email as alternate sign-in is not supported with AD FS. Deploy Staged Rollout of Seamless SSO and Pass-through Authentication to migrate away from federation

rootCause: Known bug in Azure portal SAML SSO configuration (tracked in ICM 173860105)
solution: Workaround: 1) Disable single sign-on 2) Set SSO back to SAML 3) Configure only identifiers and reply URLs first then save 4) Add sign-on URL separately and save again

rootCause: Intune device compliance or Conditional Access policies block token transfer from OWA session to Outlook mobile app.
solution: Review Intune and Conditional Access policies that may block Outlook mobile. If policies cannot be relaxed, have users sign in manually with username/password instead of QR code.

rootCause: QR code token transfer bypasses the federation server authentication flow, so MDM/device-level policies enforced by the IdP are skipped.
solution: Disable the QR code sign-in feature via Exchange PowerShell to restore username/password bootstrapping that goes through the federation server. Use Set-OrganizationConfig -MobileAppEducationEnabled $false.

rootCause: Secure Scores are generated once per day from Cosmos historical data. Changes take up to 2 days to reflect.
solution: Wait up to 2 days for the score to update. This delay is by design due to daily Cosmos data processing.

rootCause: Known bug (Office365 bug 883780) where not all Identity Actions from Office portal are synced to the Identity Secure Score blade, causing score discrepancy.

symptom: Global administrator clicks Troubleshooting and Support in Identity Secure Score blade and gets a Sad Face error page.
rootCause: The admin lacks required permissions for the redirect_uri to portal.officeppe.com, resulting in a deny during OAuth redirect. The SecureScore API returns 400 Bad Request.
solution: Collect a browser developer tools trace to confirm the permission deny. Ensure the admin has proper M365 Secure Score permissions. The issue may require PG engagement if permissions are correctly assigned.

symptom: AADSTS1002016: You are using TLS version less than 1.0, 1.1 and/or 3DES cipher. HTTP Error 426 upgrade required. Browser shows cannot connect securely due to outdated TLS settings.
rootCause: Client/application connecting to Azure AD endpoints using deprecated TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, or 3DES cipher suite (TLS_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA). Deprecated for FedRAMP compliance.
solution: Upgrade client/application to TLS 1.2. Assess dependency: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/azure/active-directory/enable-support-tls-environment. Completed: US Gov May 2022, Public cloud mid-2023, Mooncake Nov 2024.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD TLS 1.1 and TLS 1.0 deprecation

symptom: Customer requests exception/rollback for legacy TLS deprecation block on Azure AD services (eSTS, ADRS, App Proxy, PTA/HIS, MSGraph, CPIM).
rootCause: Azure AD enforces TLS 1.2 minimum at application and gateway level. Legacy TLS blocked with HTTP 426 or AADSTS1002016.
solution: Exceptions only for S500/IsStrategic with validated high impact. Steps: verify S500, CSAM validates, collect TenantID/AppID, file ICM + loop Gateway team, email AADTLSDeprecationSup@microsoft.com. No guarantee.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD TLS 1.1 and TLS 1.0 deprecation

symptom: Universal Print connector printer registration fails with StatusCode 403 Forbidden. Event ID 8 in PrintConnector/Operational log.
rootCause: (1) Printer already registered from another connector; (2) User not Global Admin or Printer Admin (wrong wid claims); (3) No Universal Print license; (4) CA policy blocking connector (Preview).
solution: Check: printer not registered elsewhere, user has correct role, UP license assigned, CA policies not blocking. Inspect access token wid claims.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD Universal Print Authentication

symptom: Azure CLI/PowerShell/SDK/IaC operations fail after mandatory MFA Phase 2 enforcement. Error may not mention MFA. PUT/PATCH/CREATE/DELETE on ARM denied.
rootCause: Azure System Policy checks MFA claim in access token for ARM control plane ops. Missing MFA flag = denied. User NOT prompted for MFA. Phase 2 started Sep 2025, public rollout Jan 2026.
solution: Enforce MFA via CA Policy/Security Defaults/Per-user MFA. Use interactive login (browser/WAM/device-code). ROPC (az login --username --password) not supported - migrate to browser or device code flow.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure Mandatory MFA Phase 2 (ARM)

symptom: ROPC (Resource Owner Password Credentials) OAuth grant flow throws exceptions after Azure mandatory MFA enforcement. UsernamePasswordCredential fails.
rootCause: ROPC inherently incompatible with MFA. ROPC-based APIs in MSAL/Azure Identity libraries cannot satisfy MFA requirement after enforcement.
solution: Migrate from ROPC to interactive auth. MSAL: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/v2-oauth-ropc#how-to-migrate-away-from-ropc. Replace UsernamePasswordCredential with InteractiveBrowserCredential or DeviceCodeCredential.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure Mandatory MFA Phase 2 (ARM)

symptom: Users added/removed from Staged Rollout groups do not immediately switch auth method. First sign-in after change still uses previous auth (federated or cloud).
rootCause: During ESTS migration, SR eligibility stored as activity store flag. Group changes do not immediately update store. First sign-in may reflect previous state.
solution: Expected during ESTS migration. User must sign in again after activity store updates. Allow propagation time.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD User Staged Rollout Migration to Cloud Auth

symptom: Nested/dynamic group membership does not work for Entra Staged Rollout. Only directly assigned users migrate to cloud auth.
rootCause: During ESTS migration, nested/dynamic group support removed for clean tenant-by-tenant migration. Only direct membership supported.
solution: Use direct group membership only during ESTS migration. Nested/dynamic support reintroduced after all tenants complete migration.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Authentication General/Azure AD User Staged Rollout Migration to Cloud Auth

symptom: CA policy configured to exclude trusted locations is bypassed when ADFS passes the InsideCorpNet claim, even after user leaves corporate network. Refresh Token retains the claim.
rootCause: InsideCorpNet claim from ADFS is cached in Refresh Token. eSTS only re-evaluates if IP changes beyond validation threshold: IPv4 validates first 3 octets, IPv6 validates first 15 octets. Minor IP changes (within last octet) do not invalidate the cached claim.
solution: Verify ADFS InsideCorpNet claim configuration. Understand that last-octet IP changes will not trigger re-evaluation. To force re-evaluation: revoke user refresh tokens. For IPv6, only the last octet (of 16) is exempt from validation.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access InsideCorpNet claim

symptom: Application calling MS Graph is unexpectedly evaluated by CA policies targeting Exchange Online or other Office 365 services, causing access blocks or MFA prompts not intended for Graph API calls.
rootCause: MS Graph scopes (e.g. Tasks.Read) are internally mapped to underlying service APIs (e.g. Exchange Online for Tasks). When a client app requests these scopes, both MS Graph and the mapped service resource AppIDs are evaluated against CA policies.
solution: Check app required MS Graph scopes in ASC (under AppID 00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000). Use Auth Troubleshooter diagnostic logs (Expert view) to identify scope-to-service mapping. Translate scope GUIDs by looking up MS Graph app. Consider using combined Office 365 app group in CA policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access MS Graph Scopes

symptom: CA policies targeting individual Office 365 apps (e.g. Exchange Online, Teams, SharePoint separately) cause inconsistent enforcement, unexpected sign-in blocks, or MFA prompts due to cross-service dependencies.
rootCause: Office 365 apps have inter-service dependencies (e.g. Teams depends on SharePoint and Exchange). Targeting individual apps in CA policies leads to unpredictable evaluation as dependent services trigger additional CA policy checks for their own resource AppIDs.
solution: Use the combined Office 365 application group in CA policies for consistent enforcement. The group includes all Office 365 AppIDs (Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, Yammer, Stream, PowerApps, etc.). Review the CA application picker recommendation. Full AppID list available in ESTS-Main Office365Apps.cs.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Office 365 app group

symptom: V1 classic MFA/location/device-based CA policies configured in Classic (AUX) portal stop being enforced or cause configuration conflicts. Three policy versions appear in Classic Policies blade when both MFA and Location+Device policies are enabled.
rootCause: Classic (AUX) portal was deprecated November 30, 2017. V1 policies must be migrated to V2 format in Azure Portal. The Classic Policies blade shows read-only view of V1 policies with V2 equivalents auto-generated but not enabled.
solution: Navigate to Conditional Access > Classic Policies blade. Phase 1: view and disable V1 policies. Phase 2: use Migrate button to auto-create V2 equivalents, then Delete old V1 policy. Verify V2 policy behavior before disabling V1. MAM policies: migrate from Intune Silverlight portal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Classic Policies (Migration)

symptom: Third-party MFA configured via Custom Controls in CA only functions after password entry, does not work for step-up authentication, and lacks integration with credential management, registration flows, and reporting.
rootCause: Custom Controls is a limited preview feature with architectural constraints. Microsoft is redesigning the feature as a new authentication extensibility framework for seamless partner integration. No new providers are being onboarded to the current Custom Controls.
solution: For new deployments: use Azure MFA built-in capabilities. For existing ADFS environments: integrate 3rd party MFA via federation server extensibility points (not Custom Controls). Existing Custom Controls users: plan migration to new authentication extensibility framework when GA. Current preview remains supported until new implementation reaches GA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Custom Controls

symptom: Users unexpectedly prompted for MFA or blocked from access when Microsoft Managed CA Policies (e.g. Multifactor authentication for all users, Block access for high-risk users) are automatically enabled after 30-day report-only period.
rootCause: Microsoft deploys Managed CA Policies in report-only mode with a 30-day countdown. If tenant admin does not disable or customize the policy within 30 days, it is automatically switched to enforced mode. Applies to both commercial and US Gov tenants.
solution: Monitor Message Center for MMP deployment notifications. Review new policies in Conditional Access blade during report-only period. Disable or customize policies before 30-day auto-enable window. If already enabled, admin can still disable or customize. Admins cannot delete MMPs but can modify exclusions and controls.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Microsoft Managed Policy

symptom: Risky user sign-in blocked with AADSTS53004 'Cannot Configure Multi-Factor Authentication Methods Due To Suspicious Activity' when User Risk CA policy with Require Password Change is enabled
rootCause: User started MFA registration but did not finish verification. The User Risk CA policy requires MFA + password change, but user has not completed MFA setup, triggering ProofUpBlockedDueToRisk.
solution: Admin temporarily excludes the user from the User Risk policy and has them sign into https://myprofile.microsoft.com with a separate User Action policy aligned with their location to finish configuring MFA. After MFA setup is complete, re-include the user in the policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access User Risk Condition and Password change Control

symptom: WhatIf tool in Conditional Access does not detect that a User Risk condition policy applies to a user
rootCause: Known bug (Bug 1007087) where WhatIf evaluation does not include User Risk condition policies in its analysis.
solution: Known issue tracked in Bug 1007087. Use Sign-in logs to verify User Risk policy evaluation instead of WhatIf. Check the Conditional Access tab in Sign-in log details for actual policy evaluation results.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access User Risk Condition and Password change Control

symptom: Error 'Require password change can only be used when policy is assigned to All cloud apps' when creating Conditional Access policy with Require Password Change grant control
rootCause: The 'Require password change' grant control is by design restricted to 'All cloud apps' target resource. It also automatically selects and locks 'Require multi-factor authentication'. Only the 'User risk' condition is supported.
solution: Select 'All cloud apps' as target resource. 'Require password change' automatically enables 'Require MFA' (unalterable). Selecting conditions other than 'User risk' will be silently reset to null when the policy is saved.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access User Risk Condition and Password change Control

symptom: Conditional Access Target Resources now shows all applications with service principals, potentially causing unintended access blocks to dependent services
rootCause: Microsoft introduced a change to expose all applications with service principals in Entra CA Target Resources. Previously only selected apps were shown. This can cause dependent service issues when policies inadvertently target apps they did not before.
solution: Review existing CA policies to ensure dependent services are not unintentionally blocked by the expanded Target Resources list. ICM escalation path: Owning Service: ESTS, Owning Team: Conditional Access.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Target Resources Now Show All Apps

symptom: Conditional Access policies using networkAccess or globalSecureAccess settings in Graph API need migration due to deprecation of these properties
rootCause: Microsoft consolidating CA Target Resources assignments from 'Cloud apps' and 'Global Secure Access' into new 'Resources' category. The networkAccess and globalSecureAccess beta API settings are deprecated.
solution: Migrate to new Global Secure Access App IDs. Editing and saving a policy in the Entra admin center automatically updates the schema. Avoid creating new policies with deprecated settings.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access Target Resources Update

symptom: 'Require approved client app' Conditional Access grant control scheduled for retirement on June 30, 2026 (MC540749). Organizations must migrate to avoid service disruption.
rootCause: Microsoft retiring the 'Require approved client app' control (originally announced March 2023) in favor of 'Require app protection policy' which provides additional security including Intune app protection policy verification.
solution: Transition to 'Require app protection policy' before June 30, 2026. Recommended: select both controls and choose 'Require one of the selected controls'. See MC540749.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Approved Client App

symptom: Error 'Policy configuration is not supported' when enabling an EAS Conditional Access policy for MAM (Require approved client app) with other Conditions also enabled
rootCause: MAM EAS policy does not support having other Conditions enabled simultaneously with Exchange ActiveSync client app condition and Require approved client app control.
solution: Set all other Conditions to 'Not configured' before enabling the EAS policy with 'Require approved client app' control.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Approved Client App

symptom: Devices already enrolled continue to prompt for device enrollment when accessing O365 Exchange Online with 'Require approved client app' and Exchange ActiveSync condition
rootCause: Native built-in Mail clients on iOS and Android are not MAM compatible. The device is enrolled but the mail app itself does not support MAM SDK.
solution: Users must use MAM-compatible apps like Microsoft Outlook. Native mail clients are blocked by design; users receive quarantine email directing to approved apps.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Approved Client App

symptom: After deleting a registered device from user account in Azure AD, user gets 'Something went wrong' error when trying to re-register the device via MAM Office app and Microsoft Authenticator
rootCause: Stale state in Microsoft Authenticator and device registration cache after admin deletes the device record from Azure AD.
solution: Close all applications completely. Re-launch the Office application - the device should register successfully on the second attempt.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Approved Client App

symptom: WhatIf tool in Conditional Access does not show policies targeting User actions (Register security information); admins think CA policies are not applied to credential registration
rootCause: WhatIf defaults to Cloud apps tab, not User actions tab. The condition label was not renamed to Cloud apps or actions in WhatIf at time of release
solution: In WhatIf, explicitly select the User action tab and check Register security information before running the query. The condition will be renamed to Cloud apps or actions by GA
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Credential Registration

symptom: Users briefly see Sorry we cannot sign you in - Please ensure cookies are enabled message when accessing converged MFA/SSPR registration flows
rootCause: Transient display issue in converged registration experience, not related to CA User action setting (Bug 686728)
solution: No action needed - this is a transient message and users will proceed to the credential management page automatically
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Credential Registration

symptom: Windows Hello for Business PIN setup or macOS Platform SSO credential registration fails/blocked during new device provisioning after April 2026 rollout
rootCause: New CA enforcement for passwordless credential registration extends to WHfB and macOS Platform SSO. CA policies targeting Register security information user action now apply during credential registration, not just sign-in
solution: Review CA policies targeting User actions > Register security information. Validate grant controls (MFA, device compliance, trusted locations). Use Report-only mode to assess impact before enforcement. Rollout begins April 2026, default enforcement May 2026
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Credential Registration

symptom: Custom Controls (e.g. Duo), Session controls, Client apps condition, and Device state condition do not work when CA policy uses Register or join devices user action
rootCause: By design - Register or join devices user action disables all Grant controls except Azure MFA and disables all Session controls, Client apps and Device state (Preview) conditions
solution: Only Azure Multi-factor authentication Grant control is supported with Register or join devices user action. Use User risk, Sign-in risk, Device Platforms, or Time conditions instead of unsupported conditions
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Device Registration

symptom: Users get Oops something went wrong or unexpected error when accessing My Apps portal while CA policy blocks All cloud apps but excludes My Apps
rootCause: My Apps depends on legacy IAMUX application launcher service which is not targetable in CA policy. Excluding My Apps from CA does not exclude IAMUX, so the CA policy still blocks the launch flow
solution: Enable new MyApps launcher (launcher.myapps.microsoft.com/api/signin) which bypasses IAMUX. Admin can toggle Enable new My Apps launch experience for conditional access in App launchers settings. For legacy Secret Store apps, update app properties to migrate to new Secret Store. MSA guest users still fall back to IAMUX (unsupported)
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for My Apps

symptom: User stuck in authentication loop when configuring CA for Protected Actions, especially after msal.js rollout (msaljs=true)
rootCause: Authentication Context is linked to a Protected Action but there is no corresponding CA policy targeting that Authentication Context, or the CA policy is set to Report-only or Disabled. The step-up capability fails and user loops
solution: Use temporary mitigation URL https://aka.ms/MSALProtectedActions to unblock. Then ensure a CA policy exists in Enabled state that targets the Authentication Context linked to the Protected Action
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Protected Actions

symptom: Admin gets Insufficient privileges to permanently delete/create/update error when performing an action protected by Authentication Context in CA
rootCause: The action is protected by an Authentication Context but no CA policy includes that Authentication Context, or the CA policy was added after user signed in so token lacks the required ACRS claim
solution: Ensure a CA policy exists that includes the Authentication Context protecting the action. If the CA policy was recently added, have the user sign out and sign back in to get a new token with the ACRS claim
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Protected Actions

symptom: VPN connection fails when combining Conditional Access for VPN with NPS Extension for Azure MFA using SSTP, L2TP, or IKEv2 connection types
rootCause: Certificate-based authentication is officially unsupported with NPS Extension for Azure MFA. Only PPTP connection type works with this configuration
solution: Use PPTP connection type. Configure EAP XML with AAD Conditional Access EKU (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.87). Add IgnoreNoRevocationCheck=1 registry key under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\RasMan\PPP\EAP\13 and reboot. Note: this combined configuration is not officially supported
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for VPN using NPS Extension for Azure MFA

symptom: VPN connectivity and Terms Of Use (TOU) blades disappeared from Azure AD portal after July 5, 2023 (ICM 403660899)
rootCause: Azure AD portal UI bug causing VPN connectivity and TOU blades to disappear from Conditional Access
solution: Access blades via direct URLs: VPN connectivity at portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_AAD_ConditionalAccess/VpnConfigurationsBlade and Terms of Use at portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_AAD_ERM/TermsOfUseSummaryBlade. Fix deployed July 24, 2023
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for Windows 10 VPN

symptom: macOS device enrollment or Conditional Access evaluation fails - device not marked as Managed/Compliant after Company Portal enrollment
rootCause: Missing Intune license assigned to user, missing Apple Push Notification Service (APNS) enrollment certificate in Intune, or device certificate deleted from Apple Login KeyChain
solution: Verify user has Intune license. Verify APNS certificate is configured in Intune. If certificate deleted from Apple Login KeyChain, un-enroll and re-enroll: Company Portal > Device > Remove, then Device > Enroll. Create macOS Compliance Policy in Intune before CA policy to avoid auto-Compliant marking
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Conditional Access for macOS

symptom: Creating or updating TokenLifetimePolicy with MaxInactiveTime, MaxAgeSingleFactor, MaxAgeMultiFactor settings fails with error: setting not supported
rootCause: As of June 19, 2020, CTL only supports Access Token lifetime. Refresh/Session Token settings are retired.
solution: Use Sign-in Frequency Session Control in CA Policy instead. Existing tenants with pre-May 2020 policies may request exception via ctlretire@microsoft.com.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Configurable Token Lifetimes

symptom: Access token lifetime varies between 60-90 minutes instead of expected fixed 60 minutes
rootCause: Since June 1, 2021, default AT lifetime jittered 60-90min (median 75min) to reduce hourly traffic spikes. Tenants without CA get 2-hour default for Teams/Office.
solution: Configure CTL policy via PowerShell to override randomness if consistent AT lifetime needed. SIF users not affected.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Configurable Token Lifetimes

symptom: User accesses resources via legacy auth (SOAP/exSTS.srf/Basic Auth) and access not revoked despite CAE policies
rootCause: CAE only works with OAuth tokens. Legacy auth outside CAE scope. FedAuth tokens contain no OAuth token.
solution: Block legacy auth via CA policy. CAE cannot protect legacy flows. Verify by decoding FedAuth token.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Continuous Access Evaluation/CAE Customer Incident Playbook

symptom: Teams calls/chat/conference continue after user IP changes despite CA location policy. CAE IP evaluation not enforced.
rootCause: Teams tokens lack capolids_latebind claim. Teams calling/messaging uses Skype tokens without CA/CAE. Only event-based revocations supported.
solution: For Teams calling/messaging, only event-based revocations work (account disabled, password changed, sessions revoked, MFA enabled, risk). CAE IP evaluation works for SPO/EXO calls.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Continuous Access Evaluation/CAE Customer Incident Playbook

symptom: After Number Match rollout (May 8, 2023), users get hourly number matching prompts instead of silent 24h GPS location pull
rootCause: Number Match for all changed approval from tap-Approve to number matching, disrupting silent 24-hour GPS pull.
solution: Fixed by Microsoft June 12-14, 2023 for Public and Gov clouds. Silent 24h location pull restored. No action needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD GPS Based Named Locations

symptom: Android devices in Developer Mode or iOS with location spoofing see Authenticator error blocking LBAC
rootCause: Authenticator Nov 2023 update detects GPS spoofing: Android via Developer Mode check, iOS 15+ via Apple API. Service-side blocks older iOS from Jan 2024.
solution: Android: disable Developer Mode and Mock Locations. iOS: remove spoofing apps. Min: Android Auth 6.2309.6329, iOS Auth 6.7.16.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD GPS Based Named Locations

symptom: CA blocks/allows incorrectly due to wrong IP-to-geolocation mapping. CA evaluates wrong country for user IP.
rootCause: Vercara (formerly Neustar/Interflow) geolocation API sometimes incorrectly maps IPs to locations.
solution: Validate at http://aka.ms/Interflowweb. Create ICM via ASC with template [ID][AUTH] IP/Location Mismatch (Rc293Q) to Interflow/SIPS API. Takes 48hrs-14 days.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD IP Location Mismatch

symptom: After Azure AD IPv6 rollout, users get unexpected CA blocks or extra MFA prompts. Sign-in logs show unrecognized IPv6 addresses.
rootCause: Client devices connect via IPv6 not in tenant Named Locations. CA location conditions miss new IPv6 ranges.
solution: Audit Named Locations, identify IPv6 via sign-in logs (https://aka.ms/azureadipv6), add egress IPv6 ranges to Named Locations.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD IPv6 enablement

symptom: CA DevicePlatforms exclusion for iOS not working with legacy auth block for EXO. What If says policy should not apply but it does.
rootCause: EAS legacy auth reports DevicePlatform as Unknown (type 14), causing exclusion to be ignored.
solution: Workaround: Two separate CA policies - one for iOS only, another for all platforms except iOS.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Protect All Protocols with Conditional Access

symptom: Sign-in logs show CA status Failure but Session status Success for blocked EAS legacy auth
rootCause: For EAS, Azure AD relies on Exchange to enforce CA. Azure AD sends block signal but still issues token. By design.
solution: By design. Exchange enforces block. Plans to update session field (Epic 1071328) but no ETA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Protect All Protocols with Conditional Access

symptom: Teams blocked when CA requires both Require approved client app AND Require app protection policy with AND operand
rootCause: AND operand requires Teams to satisfy both controls. Teams cannot satisfy app protection compliance check.
solution: Use Require one of the selected controls (OR). Azure AD prefers app protection for apps supporting compliance (OneDrive, Outlook).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Require App Protection Policy Grant Control

symptom: Require approved client app retiring June 30, 2026. Must migrate to avoid disruption.
rootCause: Microsoft deprecating in favor of Require app protection policy with Intune policy verification.
solution: Migrate to Require app protection policy by June 30, 2026. Select both with OR. See MC540749.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Require App Protection Policy Grant Control

symptom: Disabling Resilience Defaults on CA policy targeting group/role blocks ALL users during Azure AD outage, not just targeted members
rootCause: During outage, Backup Auth Service cannot evaluate group/role membership in real-time. All users denied access with Resilience Defaults disabled.
solution: Apply policy to individual users instead of groups/roles. Or leave Resilience Defaults enabled (recommended).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Resilience Defaults for Conditional Access

symptom: ROPC/non-interactive flows suddenly blocked after Security Defaults update. Users without MFA registration blocked.
rootCause: Security Defaults (Feb-Apr 2025) now enforces MFA on non-interactive flows (parity with CA). Previously allowed, now blocked.
solution: Update apps from ROPC to secure alternatives. Or temporarily disable Security Defaults. Global admins notified ~28 days before.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Security Defaults

symptom: US Gov cloud users prompted for MFA at every sign-in after April 3, 2026 Security Defaults update
rootCause: Security Defaults behavior change in US Gov: MFA at every sign-in. Cannot be changed or postponed.
solution: Expected behavior. Admins can disable Security Defaults but recommended to keep on. Consider migrating to CA.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Azure AD Security Defaults

symptom: Conditional Access policies targeting Windows Azure Service Management API (App ID: 797f4846) no longer apply to Azure DevOps sign-ins after Microsoft decoupled Azure DevOps from ARM
rootCause: Azure DevOps no longer relies on Azure Resource Manager (ARM) resource during sign-in or token refresh flows. Policies targeting ARM will not cascade to Azure DevOps.
solution: Update CA policies to explicitly include Azure DevOps (App ID: 499b84ac-1321-427f-aa17-267ca6975798) under Target resources in Entra admin center > Conditional Access > Policies.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Conditional Access Policy/Update Conditional Access Policies for Azure DevOps Sign ins

symptom: AADSTS50196 LoopDetected error during sign-in. User sees error page with StsErrorCode LoopDetected. For token endpoint requests, Invalid Grant response returned.
rootCause: Client enters authentication request loop. eSTS detects via Leaky Bucket algorithm using cookie-based fingerprint (hash of TenantId, AppId, ResourceId, UserId, GrantId). Common causes: lack of token caching, app cannot handle errors, misconfigured reply URL (http vs https).
solution: LoopDetected is a secondary failure. Use LogsMiner to load previous requests from same session. Find the first non-LoopDetected request and troubleshoot from there. Check app-side: reply URL config, token caching, error handling. Kusto: query PerRequestTableIfx by FingerprintCookieId.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/Azure AD (eSTS) Loop Breaking

symptom: Infinite sign-in loop when using Okta as federated MFA provider with Azure AD Conditional Access. Users stuck in authentication loop between Okta and Azure AD.
rootCause: Okta sign-on policy is weaker than Azure AD CA policy. Neither org-level nor app-level Okta policy requires MFA, but Azure AD CA expects completed MFA claim from Okta, causing redirect loop.
solution: Ensure Okta sign-on policy requires MFA at org or app level. Contact Okta support for known preview MFA feature issue.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/Azure AD (eSTS) Loop Breaking

symptom: ESTS sign-in page static resources (JS, CSS, images) fail to load after Edgio (formerly Verizon) CDN retirement in Jan 2025. Customer environment configured for Edgio-only CDN endpoints.
rootCause: Azure CDN provider Edgio stopped operations Jan 15, 2025. Environments restricted to Edgio-hosted endpoints lose access to ESTS CDN content.
solution: Migrate to Azure Front Door as replacement CDN. CDN product comparison: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/frontdoor/front-door-cdn-comparison
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS Content Delivery Network - ESTS CDN - ESTS Static Resources Access

symptom: ESTS sign-in page loads slowly or static resources fail. CDN endpoints (aadcdn.msftauth.net, aadcdn.msauth.net, secure.aadcdn.microsoftonline-p.com) inaccessible or delayed.
rootCause: Client-side connectivity issue or CDN resource URL blacklisted by security tools or network provider.
solution: Check URL reputation at https://www.virustotal.com/gui/home/url. Collect Fiddler trace with timeline to identify failing/delayed CDN calls. If server-side OK, issue resides on client network.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS Content Delivery Network - ESTS CDN - ESTS Static Resources Access

symptom: High latency (>2s) for guest user sign-in when guest tenant is in different geo from home tenant. StsResponseTime and DpxTotalTimeSum significantly elevated.
rootCause: DPX calls to retrieve home tenant user data must cross geo-locations. ESTS and DPX are 1:1 mapped in same fault domain. Cross-geo DPX data retrieval adds significant latency.
solution: Expected behavior. DPX SLA is 5 seconds. Check DPX/MSODS region in ASC sign-in logs (UserTenantMsodsRegionScope). Consider multi-geo data replication if legal requirements met.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS High Latency Common Issues

symptom: Higher total authentication latency (400-500ms vs 200-300ms) when user signs in from geo-location far from tenant residency. TotalGatewayTime much higher than StsResponseTime.
rootCause: AAD Gateway nearest to user proxies request to ESTS in tenant residency location. Network round-trip between Gateway and ESTS across geo-locations adds gateway-level latency.
solution: Expected behavior by design. Check GatewayDC and env_cloud_deploymentUnit in ASC logs to confirm. Consider data replication to other geo-locations if needed.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS High Latency Common Issues

symptom: Windows 11 Operating Systems showing up as Windows 10 in Microsoft Entra Sign-in Logs.
rootCause: eSTS not correctly parsing UserAgent string. UserAgent with OS version >= 10.0.22000 should be identified as Windows 11 but reported as Windows 10.
solution: Known issue being resolved by ESTS team (Work Item 1587325). Status: Resolving.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS Known Issues(Draft)

symptom: User signs in via WHfB but gets blocked by CA Authentication Strength policy. Error says password not satisfied despite WHfB being an allowed method.
rootCause: When previous PRT has MFA claim from different method (e.g., push notification), the MFA claim in NGC (WHfB) was dropped. Auth strength requires Password + Push Notification combination.
solution: Fixed in ESTS: merge MFA containers in previous refresh tokens with user credentials like WHfB. See ESTS-Main PR 10740696. ICM: 521412781.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS Known Issues(Draft)

symptom: HTTP 429 errors from ESTS endpoints (login.microsoftonline.com). Application requests being throttled during normal usage.
rootCause: Exceeding ESTS throttling limits. Tenant+App: 500-2000 RPS depending on app level. Application across all tenants: 20000-200000 RPS. Thresholds applied per region. AAD Gateway has additional IP-based throttling.
solution: Share scenario details and expected volume with ESTS team. Optimize app to reduce load. If confirmed normal usage, engage ESTS Incident Triage (Owning service: ESTS, Team: Incident Triage).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS Throttling Service

symptom: Third-party apps in Azure Government (Fairfax) failing auth after instance-unaware authentication deprecation. Apps using commercial login.microsoftonline.com instead of .us endpoints.
rootCause: Microsoft retiring instance-unaware authentication for Azure Government for FedRAMP/DoD IL45 compliance. 3P apps must use government-specific endpoints.
solution: Update app auth to use login.microsoftonline.us endpoints for Fairfax. See: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity-platform/authentication-national-cloud. Route cases to instanceunaware@microsoft.com.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD ESTS/ESTS_Instance_Unaware_Authentication_Deprecation

symptom: Admin cannot delete applications disabled by Microsoft (disabledByMicrosoftStatus set). Admin requests deletion of disabled app in Enterprise Apps.
rootCause: By design: disabled applications cannot be deleted to prevent re-instantiation, closing a security loophole.
solution: Do NOT file ICM for deletion requests. Track in shared Excel spreadsheet. If disabled app causes functional problems, then file ICM.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: Service principal risk condition shows 'Not available' when creating Conditional Access Policy with workload identities selected.
rootCause: Requires 'All cloud apps' under Cloud apps or actions. Selecting specific apps makes condition unavailable.
solution: Select Cloud apps or actions > All cloud apps when creating CA policy targeting workload identities.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: Legitimate application config changes trigger 'Suspicious Sign-ins' risk detection for workload identities (false positive).
rootCause: Suspicious Sign-ins detection uses behavioral baseline (2-60 days) for IP/ASN, target resource, user agent, credential type. Legitimate changes match same pattern.
solution: Admin can dismiss risk for the service principal. Future: specific reason codes will distinguish legitimate changes.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: RepMAP disabled 'Tutorial Sample App' (6731de76-14a6-49ae-97bc-6eba6914391e) triggering high-risk 'Malicious Application' event across ~40K tenants.
rootCause: Microsoft RepMAP team proactively disabled app whose live secret was exposed in public OAuth documentation. Disablement triggered Application Disablement Action in AADIP.
solution: Share prepared customer communication. Customer should review privileges granted and check logs. App cannot be deleted (by design).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: Account lockout with ErrorCode 'IdsLocked', RamRecommender=17, RamRecommendedAction=100, RamAdhocDebuggingInfo contains 'LABlock'. Legacy auth blocked.
rootCause: Identity Protection automatically blocks high-risk legacy auth sessions for High Risk users with high real-time risk and no MFA interrupt.
solution: Dismiss user risk to stop blocking. Best practice: stop using legacy authentication entirely.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Legacy Auth Blocking

symptom: 'Number of users protected' metric in Identity Protection dashboard shows inaccurate remediation activity counts.
rootCause: Known issue in dashboard metric calculation during initial rollout.
solution: Fix has been rolled out. If still inaccurate, escalate to Identity Protection Service/Triage.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Entra ID Protection New Dashboard

symptom: Identity Protection Risk Management Agent: 'Trigger one time run' and 'Remove Agent' buttons greyed out for Security Administrators.
rootCause: Security Administrator role not added to Security Copilot Owner role in standalone portal. Global Admins inherit this automatically.
solution: Add Security Administrator role to Security Copilot Owner role in Security Copilot standalone portal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Identity Risk Management Agent

symptom: Error 0x51f running 'klist get' for Azure SQL MI or Azure File Share. Fiddler tool installed on system.
rootCause: Fiddler installs proxy config that interferes with Kerberos KDC proxy communication.
solution: Uninstall Fiddler and delete registry keys under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\iphlpsvc\Parameters\ProxyMgr (and ControlSet001/002).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Error 0x51f / 0x2ee5 (ERROR_INTERNET_INVALID_URL) with KDC proxy for Azure AD Kerberos. KDC proxy URL misconfigured in GPO.
rootCause: Missing space at end of Kerberos URL in KDC proxy GPO, or invisible/extra characters in URL.
solution: Verify KDC proxy URL format: must have space after '/kerberos' before closing. Validate no invisible characters.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Error 0x51f mounting Azure File Share with per-user MFA Enforce enabled on user account.
rootCause: Per-user MFA 'Enforce' mode blocks Kerberos ticket acquisition for Azure File Share. No workaround for Enforce mode.
solution: Disable per-user MFA 'Enforce' option. Use Conditional Access policies for MFA instead.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Error 0x51f with 'ProxyHelperClientGetProxyForUrl failed: 1753' in Kerberos ETL. WinHTTP or IP Helper service not running.
rootCause: WinHTTP Web Proxy Auto-Discovery Service (WinHttpAutoProxySvc) or IP Helper service (iphlpsvc) stopped/disabled.
solution: Ensure WinHttpAutoProxySvc and iphlpsvc services are running.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: After rotating Azure AD Kerberos trust key (Set-AzureAdKerberosServer -RotateServerKey), Azure File Share access via referral tickets breaks for ~23 hours.
rootCause: Entra ID activates new keys after 24h by default, causing mismatch and Kerberos ticket decryption failures (0xc000009a, KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED).
solution: Delete trust and recreate: Remove-AzureADKerberosServerTrustedDomainObject then Set-AzureAdKerberosServer -SetupCloudTrust. Avoid rotation until fix released.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Set-AzureAdKerberosServer fails: 'missing required properties. Property: UserAccount.SecondaryKrbTgtNumber Value:0'.
rootCause: $domainCred lacks sufficient privileges. Must be Domain Admins (root domain) AND Enterprise Admins (forest).
solution: Ensure $domainCred is member of Domain Admins in root domain AND Enterprise Admins for the forest.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Set-AzureADKerberosServer fails: 'The user has insufficient access rights' when creating AzureADKerberos object.
rootCause: Account lacks SeEnableDelegationPrivilege on DCs, needed to set TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION. GPO may override default.
solution: Check gpresult for 'Enable computer and user accounts to be trusted for delegation' policy. Add builtin\Administrators to winning GPO.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Set-AzureAdKerberosServer fails: 'Strong authentication is required for this operation' (DirectoryOperationException). Root domain DC requires LDAP signing.
rootCause: Mismatch: ldapserverintegrity=2 on root DC vs ldapClientIntegrity=0 on client. DC requires signed LDAP binds.
solution: Set LdapClientIntegrity=2 on client registry (HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LDAP). Configure GPO: Network security > LDAP client signing > Require signing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Azure File Share mount fails with error 50076 'multi-factor authentication required' when CA policy targets Storage SPN.
rootCause: Conditional Access policy applied to Azure Storage Service Principal (SPN) requires MFA which cannot be satisfied during Kerberos ticket acquisition.
solution: Exclude Azure Storage SPN from the Conditional Access policy.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Azure File Share mount fails with AADSTS50105 when 'Assignment required' enabled on Storage Service Principal in Enterprise Apps.
rootCause: SP used by AAD only for Kerberos ticket issuance, not Azure Files authorization. Enabling assignment required blocks ticket issuance.
solution: Disable 'Assignment required' on Storage Account SP. Use VNet ACLs, RBAC, file ACLs for access control.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Intermittent Kerberos failures acquiring CloudTgt in HAADJ environments. Event 100: SPN not registered for krbtgt/kerberos.microsoftonline.com.
rootCause: Windows platform issue: expiring cloud TGT triggers inappropriate Kerberos referral from on-prem to AAD.
solution: Workaround: set up Kerberos incoming trust from Entra ID to on-prem AD. Check internal wiki for official fix.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Set-AzureAdKerberosServer fails on Windows Server 2025: '2.2.10.6 Primary:Kerberos-Newer-Keys. ServiceCredentials MUST be zero'.
rootCause: Bug in AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement module versions older than 2.4.71.0 on Windows Server 2025.
solution: Update to AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement module v2.4.71.0+ from PowerShell Gallery.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: 'Could not load file or assembly Microsoft.Online.PasswordSynchronization.Rpc.dll' when using AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement module.
rootCause: VC++ 2013 Runtime (x64) not installed. Module auto-install fails due to deprecated -Path parameter in newer VcRedist module.
solution: Update to AzureADHybridAuthenticationManagement v2.4.71.0. Or manually install VC++ 2013 x64 Runtime.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Set-AzureAdKerberosServer fails: 'Collision setting top level names on trust KERBEROS.MICROSOFTONLINE.COM'.
rootCause: 'windows.net' top-level name already in Name Suffix Routing of another AD trust, conflicting with Azure AD Kerberos trust.
solution: Open AD Domains And Trusts > check trust properties > Name Suffix Routing > remove entry containing 'windows.net' from conflicting trust.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Azure File Share access lost after 10+ hours on Entra ID Joined device (e.g., FSLogix VHD in AVD). Error 0xc000018b.
rootCause: Expiring cloud TGT triggers inappropriate Kerberos referral. Applies when both Cloud TGT + on-prem TGT present, Entra ID Joined with cloud trust.
solution: Workaround: set up Kerberos incoming trust. Check internal wiki for official resolution.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: Multiple Kerberos incoming trusts across different Entra tenants to same on-prem AD domain is not supported.
rootCause: By design: Azure AD Kerberos does not support syncing one AD DS to multiple AAD tenants via Kerberos trust.
solution: Not supported. Feature request ADO 2372314. Use separate AD domains for different Entra tenants.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Kerberos/Azure AD Kerberos - Known Issues

symptom: In Azure Free tenants, toggling Security defaults to On in the MFA Wizard takes immediate effect before the wizard is completed. Admin cannot re-run the wizard from start after enabling.
rootCause: Security defaults toggle is applied immediately when switched On in the wizard UI, not deferred to wizard completion.
solution: Navigate to Properties blade > Manage security defaults link to disable Security defaults, then re-run the wizard from start.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA Wizard

symptom: MFA Server stops servicing user sign-ins after TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecation (Feb 28, 2022). MFA authentication requests fail silently.
rootCause: Azure MFA backend deprecated TLS 1.0/1.1 connections. MFA Server communicating using old TLS versions can no longer reach the backend.
solution: Verify customer is on the TLS 1.0/1.1 deprecation impact list. Upgrade MFA Server and OS to support TLS 1.2. Ensure .NET Framework 4.6+ and registry settings enforce TLS 1.2.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA server

symptom: Customer still using on-premises Azure MFA Server which was deprecated September 30, 2024. New activations blocked since July 1, 2019.
rootCause: MFA Server is deprecated. No new deployments allowed. Existing servers continue to work but will eventually lose support.
solution: Migrate to cloud Azure MFA using the MFA Server Migration Utility. Resources: (1) learn.microsoft.com/entra/identity/authentication/how-to-migrate-mfa-server-to-azure-mfa (2) MFA Server Migration Utility how-to doc (3) Contact mfaservermigration@microsoft.com for end-to-end assistance. Include LQR template in case closure email.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD MFA server

symptom: VPN authorization fails after number matching rollout (May 8, 2023) for organizations using RADIUS protocols other than PAP. AuthZOptCh event log shows: "Challenge requested in Authentication Ext for User ... with state ..."
rootCause: NPS extension does not support Number Matching, Rich Context, or Geographic Location awareness. Non-PAP RADIUS protocols cannot handle the number matching challenge flow.
solution: Update NPS extension to version 1.2.2216.1 or later. Users will receive One-Time Passcodes (OTP) via SMS push notification as fallback. Ensure RADIUS uses PAP protocol for best compatibility. Alternatively, modify registry for TOTP per MS Learn NPS extension guidance.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD NPS Extension for Azure MFA

symptom: SMS cannot be used as first factor or for self-service password reset in Entra External ID tenants. Voice-based MFA also not supported. Authentication strengths CA policy cannot enforce SMS as MFA method.
rootCause: SMS is only supported as a second-factor MFA method in External ID. SSPR and first-factor scenarios require Email OTP.
solution: Use Email OTP for SSPR. SMS is only for MFA second factor. Cannot disable EOTP at tenant level if SSPR is needed. Voice MFA not supported in External ID.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD Phone SMS as an MFA method

symptom: User in legacy MFA blocked list can still sign in with password-only for resources not protected by MFA, or with FIDO2/WHfB/other strong auth methods not controlled by SAS.
rootCause: Legacy MFA block list only prevents MFA authentication (registration or sign-in with traditional MFA methods). It does not block other authentication paths like FIDO2 or WHfB.
solution: Use new Report Suspicious Activity feature (integrates with Identity Protection) for more comprehensive coverage. With AAD P2, risk-based CA policies can drive self-remediation. With AAD P1, use Risk Detections API to detect "User reported suspicious activity" and disable user account or force password change.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure AD Report Suspicious Activity (MFA Fraud Report)

symptom: Double MFA prompt when Azure MFA Adapter is configured as 2nd factor for O365 Relying Party in ADFS 2016.
rootCause: Azure MFA Adapter is intended for RPs other than O365, since Azure MFA is already enforced at the AAD service level for O365/cloud apps federated to ADFS.
solution: Do not use Azure MFA Adapter for O365 RP. Use Azure MFA enforced at the service level (Conditional Access or Security Defaults) for O365.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Adapter for ADFS 2016

symptom: User using Azure MFA as primary authentication in ADFS 2016 gets prompted for MFA again when accessing cloud app requiring MFA.
rootCause: Azure MFA as primary auth in Standard mode (no PIN) does not emit the multipleauthn claim, so the MFA requirement is not satisfied at the cloud service level.
solution: Use Azure MFA as secondary authentication instead of primary. PIN mode for primary auth (when available) will emit the multipleauthn claim and satisfy MFA requirement.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Adapter for ADFS 2016

symptom: Azure MFA Adapter for ADFS 2016 fails when HTTP proxy is configured on the AD FS server.
rootCause: Azure MFA Adapter uses WinHTTP proxy settings, not the system/IE proxy configuration.
solution: Run CMD as admin: "netsh winhttp set proxy server:port" to configure WinHTTP proxy. Verify with "netsh winhttp show proxy".
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Adapter for ADFS 2016

symptom: Customer unable to upload HOTP-based hardware OATH tokens to Azure MFA. Only TOTP tokens with 30 or 60 second intervals are accepted.
rootCause: Azure MFA only supports OATH TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) with 30 or 60 second refresh intervals. OATH HOTP (HMAC-based) is not supported.
solution: Use TOTP-based hardware tokens with 30 or 60 second code lifetime. Contact token vendor for TOTP-compatible devices. Tokens must use Base32 encoding and numeric-only codes.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Hardware OATH Tokens

symptom: Hardware OATH token upload fails due to secret key exceeding 128 character limit. Affects certain vendors like Gemalto.
rootCause: Current secret key limit is 128 characters. Tokens with longer secret keys are rejected during CSV upload.
solution: Verify secret key length is within 128 characters. If vendor provides longer keys, contact vendor for shorter key format or consider alternative token vendors.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Hardware OATH Tokens

symptom: Cannot block registration of authenticator apps as software OATH tokens even after unchecking legacy MFA settings (Notification through mobile app / Verification code from mobile app or hardware token).
rootCause: SSPR still has Mobile app code and/or Mobile app notification checked in Authentication methods blade, and migration state is Pre-migration. Legacy policies for both MFA and SSPR control registration independently.
solution: Also uncheck SSPR authentication method settings for Mobile app code/notification, or complete the migration from Pre-migration to Authentication Method Policies (set migration status to Complete).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD MFA/Azure MFA Software OATH Tokens

symptom: Admin cannot delete an OAuth application disabled by Microsoft (disabledByMicrosoftStatus set). Delete option is unavailable in Enterprise Apps.
rootCause: By design: preventing deletion avoids a security loophole where disabled apps could be re-instantiated in the tenant.
solution: Do not file ICM for this. If the disabled app causes a functional problem, file ICM to PG. If admin just does not want to see it, track via PG Excel spreadsheet. Deletion of Microsoft-disabled apps is not supported.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: When creating a Conditional Access Policy with Workload identities selected, the Service principal risk condition shows as Not available and is unmanageable.
rootCause: All cloud apps is not selected under Cloud apps or actions when Workload identities is chosen as the identity type.
solution: Select Cloud apps or actions and choose All cloud apps to make the Service principal risk condition available.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureAD/AzureAD:/GeneralPages/AAD/AAD Authentication/Azure AD Identity Protection/Azure AD Identity Protection for Workload Identities

symptom: Application Tutorial Sample App (AppId 6731de76-14a6-49ae-97bc-6eba6914391e) appears disabled in tenant with Identity Protection Malicious Application risk event. ~40K tenants affected.
rootCause: Microsoft RepMap team globally disabled the app because its live client secret was exposed in public OAuth documentation. The disablement triggered an Application Disablement Action risk event in AADIP.
