symptom: Known issue where if customers try to use ADFS Authentication through an ILB/VPN, they are unable to. Topology:Local Client -> VPN -> ILB -> ADFS server(s)
rootCause: The issue is due to a problem with how ILBs handle the jumbo packets sent by ADFS servers as SSL "Server Hello. Certificates". Once they reach the Azure Gateway, they are not fragmented properly so they do not make it across the VPN tunnel.
solution: Solution is to reduce the MTU packet size on the ADFS servers by executing:netsh interface ipv4 show subinterfacesnetsh interface ipv4 set subinterface "Sub-interface name from previous command" mtu=1350 store=persistentYou can point customers to this solution on the Azure Diagnostics blade:
sourceRef: ContentIdea#64141

symptom: Microsoft is releasing this security advisory to inform customers that a new version of Azure Active Directory (AD) Connect is available that addresses an Important security vulnerability. The update addresses a vulnerability that could allow elevation of privilege if Azure AD Connect Password writeback is misconfigured during enablement. An attacker who successfully exploited this vulnerability could reset passwords and gain unauthorized access to arbitrary on-premises AD privileged user accounts. The issue is addressed in the latest version (1.1.553.0) of Azure AD Connect by not allowing arbitrary password reset to on-premises AD privileged user accounts.
rootCause: The issue is addressed in the latest version (1.1.553.0) of Azure AD Connect by not allowing arbitrary password reset to on-premises AD privileged user accounts.
solution: See https://technet.microsoft.com/library/security/4033453.aspxInstall the 1.1.553.0 version of AAD Connect
sourceRef: ContentIdea#64355

symptom: Abstract The AAD Connect client supports auto-upgrading from older to newer versions of the identity sync client. The AAD Connect product group is continually increasing the scope of AAD Connect configurations that eligible for auto-update. On or around July 21st, version 1.1.561 of AAD Connect was released for Auto-Upgrade which exposed a DCOM API dependency which causes an access violation in the auto-update process which subsequently fails to complete. That auto-update process has since been halted. Version 1.1.561 has now been removed from AutoUpgrade and a new version of AAD Connect will be pushed out to fix broken systems. Customers that open support calls for sync failures caused by the auto-update process should confirm a scenario match using signatures in the symptoms section of this article which can be corrected using the steps in the resolution section. Impacted tenants can be broken down into 1 of 3 scenarios:Scheduler is suspended. No object synchronization is occurring Scheduler is suspended. Customer also has PHS and PHS is not syncing Scheduler is not suspended. Object synchronization is fine. But PHS is disabled Symptoms Customers may call Microsoft Support in and after July 2017 stating that their AAD Connect suddenly stopped synchronizing for no apparent reason.Customer may have received Unhealthy Identity Synchronization Notification emails from MSOnlineServicesTeam@MicrosoftOnline.com The Synchronization Service Manager UI indicates that regular sync cycles stopped around July, 21st, 2017Checking Sync Scheduler status using in Get-ADSyncScheduler in PowerShell confirms that sync is suspended indicated by SchedulerSuspended being set to True and NextSyncCycleStartTimeInUTC, whichidentifies the next sync cycle, having the date in the past when AAD Connect stopped synchronizing.Additional signatures that confirm a scenario match:Looking up at the logs under %ProgramData%\AADConnect, we can confirm that AutoUpgrade was attempted on the same date that server stopped synchronizingThe latest SyncEngine-AutoUpgrader-date-time.log shows that version 1.1.561.0 was installed by AutoUpgrade. Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : ========================================================================== Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : Sync Engine Auto Upgrade Starting: 07/21/2017 13:30:52 Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : ========================================================================== Azure AD Connect Upgrade Verbose: 903 : Upgrading AADConnect to version: 1.1.561.0Control Panel/Programs and Features or Sync UI Help | About shows AAD Connect version 1.1.561.0 is installedBut (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,Value indicates that Server Configuration version under Global Settings is from an old buildNote: In this case there�s a mismatch between AAD Connect synchronization services version (1.1.561) and the server configuration version (1.1.281). Launching the Wizard, shows a pending Upgrade, meaning that latest AAD Connect setup wizard was not run yet. Application event logs around the date when server stopped synchronizing indicate an access violation exception error caused by AAD Connect Upgrader process: Log Name: Application Source: .NET Runtime Date: 7/21/2017 1:35:02 PM Event ID: 1026 Task Category: None Level: Error Keywords: Classic User: N/A Computer: O365SYN5.O365.NuAlex.net Description: Application: Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.Synchronization.Upgrader.exe Framework Version: v4.0.30319 Description: The process was terminated due to an unhandled exception. Exception Info: System.AccessViolationException Stack: at MmsServerRCW.IMMSServer2.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid ByRef, Int32 ByRef, Int32 ByRef) at MmsServerRCW.IMMSServer2.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid ByRef, Int32 ByRef, Int32 ByRef) at Microsoft.DirectoryServices.MetadirectoryServices.UI.WebServices.MMSWebService.GetSchedulerConnectorOverride(System.Guid) at Microsoft.IdentityManagement.PowerShell.Cmdlet.SetADSyncSchedulerConnectorOverrideCmdlet.ProcessRecord() at System.Management.Automation.CommandProcessor.ProcessRecord() at System.Management.Automation.CommandProcessorBase.DoExecute() at System.Management.Automation.Internal.PipelineProcessor.SynchronousExecuteEnumerate(System.Object, System.Collections.Hashtable, Boolean) at System.Management.Automation.Runspaces.LocalPipeline.InvokeHelper() at System.Management.Automation.Runspaces.LocalPipeline.InvokeThreadProc() at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.RunInternal(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object, Boolean) at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.Run(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object, Boolean) at System.Threading.ExecutionContext.Run(System.Threading.ExecutionContext, System.Threading.ContextCallback, System.Object) at System.Threading.ThreadHelper.ThreadStart()
rootCause: In July 2017, the AAD Connect team expanded the scope of AAD Connect sync clients eligible for AutoUpgrade. However, the AAD Connect Upgrader binaries that are downloaded by the AutoUpgrade process took a DLL dependency from AAD Connect version 1.1.484 that causes the Upgrader to crash when trying to upgrade older versions of AAD Connect where the updated DLL is not present. Since the AutoUpgrade scope has been increased to include more AAD Connect configurations, AutoUpgrade fails for older versions of AAD Connect installations which caught many older builds that Customers didn�t upgrade for many months. Customers that were already in version 1.1.484 or later were not affected by this issue.In summary, synchronization fails after 1.1.557 and later versions of AAD Connect attempt to auto-upgrade versions 1.1.443 and earlier of AAD Connect, for the following root cause:A new DCOM API change was introduced in build 1.1.484.Auto-Upgrade took a dependency on the DCOM API change in build 1.1.557 and later.As part of the process of auto-upgrading to build 1.1.557 and later, the Upgrader process attempts to query DCOM.If AAD Connect is being upgraded from more recent versions 1.1.484 and after, DCOM DLL already contains the Upgrader dependency and the upgrade process completes correctly without issue.However, in cases where the old DCOM DLL loaded in memory is from a build prior to 1.1.484, then the API is incompatible and AccessViolation exception occurs.
solution: AAD Connect team already removed the latest version from AutoUpgrade so there�s no more instances of this issue and we are planning/testing a new AAD Connect version that will be pushed by AutoUpgrade and fix the issue for all broken customers. In the meanwhile, many customers may call Support to address this issue so here�s the steps required to mitigate the problem.Launch AAD Connect Wizard and click Upgrade � should only ask for the AAD credentials.After completing the Upgrade, Exit the Wizard and close any PowerShell windows.Confirm again the Global Settings from PowerShell and it should show the Server Configuration version updated to 1.1.561.0: (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,ValueOpen AAD Connect Wizard again and go to View current configuration IMPORTANT: Check if features like PasswordSync or Password Writeback are ok. If there�s any feature that shows as Disabled that was previously enabled, Customer needs to go ahead and enable these features again.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#65234

symptom: AbstractThere is an issue that is causing Automatic Upgrade to not work correctly for Azure AD Connect servers with version 1.1.443.0 and below. Azure AD Connect servers affected by this issue are left in one of the following states: The server is partially upgraded. The scheduler is suspended, and no automatic synchronization cycle occurs. The server is upgraded. The scheduler is enabled, and object changes are synchronized correctly to Azure AD. However, Password Sync feature and/or Password Writeback feature are disabled after upgrade. SymptomsTo determine whether your Azure AD Connect server is affected by this issue: 1. Determine whether Automatic Upgrade recently attempted to upgrade your server by looking at the log files under folder %ProgramData%\AADConnect. The presence of log files with title SyncEngine-AutoUpgrader-[Date]-[Time].log indicates that Automatic Upgrade occurred and when. 2. If Automatic Upgrade has occurred in the month of July 2017, verify whether Automatic Upgrade has left the server in partially upgraded state: o Start the Azure AD Connect wizard. If the server is partially upgraded, you will be prompted to upgrade your Azure AD Connect server. o Compare the binary version of the Azure AD Connect with its server configuration version. During automatic upgrade, the binaries are upgraded first, followed by the server configuration. If they are different, the server is partially-upgraded. To check the server binary version, go to Control Panel Add/Remove Program Files and look for Microsoft Azure AD Connect. To check the server configuration version, run cmdlet (Get-ADSyncGlobalSettings).Parameters | select Name,Value and look for Microsoft.Synchronize.ServerConfigurationVersion: o Check whether the Sync Scheduler is in suspended state by running cmdlet Get-ADSyncScheduler. If the SchedulerSuspended property is true, the scheduler is suspended. 3. If the server isnt in partially upgraded state, and you have previously enabled Password Synchronization feature and Password Writeback feature on the server, verify that the features are enabled by selecting *View current configuration* in the Azure AD Connect wizard:
rootCause: There is a problem in AAD Connect Upgrader that causes the process Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.Synchronization.Upgrader.exe to terminate due to an unhandled exception, leaving the auto-upgrade setup incomplete.
solution: If the server is left in partially-upgraded state by Automatic Upgrade:Perform a manual, in-place upgrade on the server by starting the Azure AD Connect wizard and clicking Upgrade. For details on how to perform in-place upgrade, refer to this article.Once the Upgrade is complete, repeat the steps described earlier to confirm that the server is no longer in partially upgraded state.If you have previously enabled Password Synchronization feature and/or Password Writeback feature, verify that the feature remains enabled after the Upgrade is complete.If the features have been disabled by Automatic Upgrade, you must manually enable them by selecting *Customize synchronization options* in the Azure AD Connect wizard:
sourceRef: ContentIdea#65324

symptom: AbstractThis article explains mitigation steps and provides some best practices to create a custom Sync Rule in Synchronization Rules Editor when the following error occurs:"Object reference not set to an instance of an object" SymptomsWhen creating a new Sync Rule in Synchronization Rules Editor UI, error "Object reference not set to an instance of an object" appears after clicking "Add" on the last Sync Rule configuration page.
rootCause: Normally, when adding a new custom Sync Rule by clicking in "Add new rule", it's not mandatory to provide a Tag string, but due to a bug in AAD Connect 1.1.561, the Synchronization Rules Editor will throw the error shown above if Tag textbox is empty. The Tag is present on the first Sync Rule configuration page as shown below.This issue is limited to the Synchronization Rules Editor UI as PowerShell cmdlet 'New-ADSyncRule' works correctly without specifying a Tag.The fix for this bug has been checked-in and will be included in the Upcoming AAD Connect August Release.
solution: There's two possible workarounds available for this issue:Create the new custom Sync Rule using Powershell New-ADSyncRule cmdletORType a custom Tag string on the first Sync Rule configuration page (Description).Best PracticeMicrosoft recommends you create this tag with a name that is unique to your company, identifies the purpose of the rule, and indicates a version. For example, a rule that performs custom filtering of users might be called: Contoso.CloudFilteredUser.001.WARNING: All rules starting with Microsoft.* are subject to deletion or revision during upgrades. To guarantee custom rules are preserved they must not start with a Microsoft prefix.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#65893

symptom: Abstract This article describes the symptoms when file "C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\PersistedState.xml" is missing or is corrupted. The PersistedState.xml file holds important data about current AAD Connect Synchronization settings and AD FS integration that is necessary to run AAD Connect Wizard. If this file is deleted or corrupted, the Wizard will completely lose out current server configuration state or may throw the exceptions errors described below. The purpose of this article is to show how to identity and recover a broken AAD Connect Wizard due to the PersistedState file being corrupted or missing by restoring a backup of the PersistedState file so that the Wizard can work again.SymptomsThere are 3 different symptoms whether the PersistedState file - "C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\PersistedState.xml" - is missing a) and b), or is corrupted c).a) Launching the Wizard when PersistedState file is missingThe wizard won�t find the file and therefore thinks AAD Connect is being set up for the first time. In this case, the user will have the same experience as they did when first launching it.b) An AAD Connect Upgrade when PersistedState file is missingAfter installing AzureADConnect.msi the Wizard shows the �Upgrade Azure Active Directory Connect� a page. If the Customer goes ahead and clicks the Upgrade button, setup will fail with the errors below: Trace log error: System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null. Parameter name: state[01:08:36.767] [ 12] [INFO ] Calling to get the last dir sync time for the current user[01:08:36.997] [ 12] [ERROR] A terminating unhandled exception occurred.Exception Data (Raw): System.AggregateException: One or more errors occurred. ---> System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null.Parameter name: state at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElementInternal(PersistedStateElement element, PersistedStateContainer state) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElement(PersistedStateElement element) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.OneADWizard.UI.WizardPages.AzureTenantPageViewModel.ValidateCredentials() at System.Threading.Tasks.Task.Execute() --- End of inner exception stack trace ------> (Inner Exception #0) System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null.Parameter name: state at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElementInternal(PersistedStateElement element, PersistedStateContainer state) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.Types.PersistedState.MicrosoftOnlinePersistedStateProvider.SetStateElement(PersistedStateElement element) at Microsoft.Online.Deployment.OneADWizard.UI.WizardPages.AzureTenantPageViewModel.ValidateCredentials() at System.Threading.Tasks.Task.Execute()<--- Wizard Upgrade error: ArgumentNullException � Value cannot be null. Parameter name: statec) When PersistedState file is corruptedWhether the user is launching the Wizard or upgrading AAD Connect, when the Wizard launches while the PersistedState file is corrupted, the user will immediately see an error: InvalidOperationException � There is an error in XML document (x, y).
rootCause: In the AAD Connect upgrade path, the PersistedState file is written over with a blank copy and subsequently filled with contents later on. However, if the application hits an unexpected error before the second write happens, the file will never be updated, and the server is left with a blank file. This behavior has been corrected in the August release of AAD Connect. There were also some instances where Customers deleted this file or folder by mistake.
solution: In order to fix this issue, Customer should restore the file/folder from the latest server backup. If customer does not have a backup of the disk or if the backup is not up-to-date, follow the steps below to use to restore from a previous backup file:a) Go to the folder where the file exists, i.e. C:\ProgramData\AADConnect\, there should be backup file(s) named �Backup-PersistedState-[timestamp].xml�. e.g.: Backup-PersistedState-20170825-010641.xmlb) Please move off the corrupted PersistedState.xml file so that we can analyze it to determine what happened (if possible).c) Locate the latest backup file and rename it to �PersistedState.xml�.d) Customer should be able to launch the Wizard to execute a task or proceed with the Upgrade. However, any state changes after the last backup file was created will be lost. NOTE: If you got a corrupted PersistedState.xml from a Customer please sent it over by email to Jody Hendrix <jodyh@microsoft.com> for root cause analysis, referring this Article ID 4040732.

solution:
First confirm:
  
  
    A.
    There are no missing antivirus exceptions per 
    
      
      
      https://support.office.com/en-us/article/certain-folders-may-have-to-be-excluded-from-antivirus-scanning-when-you-use-file-level-antivirus-software-in-sharepoint-01cbc532-a24e-4bba-8d67-0b1ed733a3d9
      
    
     
  
  
    B.   The AD RMS role is not installed on any of the SharePoint servers or on the RMS server.  It is not supported to run AD RMS role locally on a SharePoint server with the IRM client configured and installed when using Azure Active Directory Rights Management.  Generally, if you find yourself in this state, it is RECOMMENDED to build out a new server and add it to the farm without the AD RMS role local installed.  In this instance, SharePoint 2013 is unsupported for anything other than security updates.  
  
  
     
  
  
    1.
    Check for missing registry keys:
  
  
    If the keys are missing, attach the installation media and run a repair and reboot or 
  
  
    copy missing registry key from a test server: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Shared Tools\Web Server Extensions\15.0\IrmProtectors
  
  
  
    2.
    Connect to Azure Active Directory Service and run the following commands: 
  
  
    To connect to the Azure Active Directory Service, we updated the Windows Framework 5.1 and rebooted per:  
    
      
      
      https://gallery.technet.microsoft.com/office/PowerShell-Install-Module-388e47a1#content
      
    
     , then ran these commands:
  
  
    a.
    Run these commands locally on each server, CA and WFE: Update the email address with the email address of the O365 account used to configure the Microsoft Rights Management Connector Administration tool on server with the AADRMS installed.
  
  
    b.
    Install-Module -Name AADRM
  
  
    c.
    (Get-Module AADRM �ListAvailable).Version
  
  
    d.
    Connect-AadrmService
  
  
    e.
    #enter creds for AAD global admin
  
  
    f.
    Add-AadrmRoleBasedAdministrator -EmailAddress mebajric@melomel.onmicrosoft.com -Role "ConnectorAdministrator"
  
  
    g.
    Get-AADRMConfiguration  
  
  
    3.
    After running the AADRM commands above, try downloading a document.  In other instances, we were able to decrypt office documents upon download successfully 
    .  After trying to download a document, the values for the MSIPC registry key should be populated including connector info, user sids, authtypes and template info.
  
  
    4.
    If documents are still erroring upon download, review the More Information section below.

symptom:
KeyVault
renewal certificate admin was unable to see the new certificate and
it was showing old certificate instead.

 

Backgroud: Since
customer was unable to see new (renewed) app service certificate in Keyvault.
App team helped customer download the new certificate from the Keyvault and
upload it in Application Gateway. However customer was still unable to see the
new certificate.
rootCause:
Missing Permission on
Services Principle which is responsible for performing this action to show new
certificate under KeyVault.
solution:
Ran Jarvis Query targeting the Tenant ID and the Impacted
     KeyVault. As we can see in below screenshot there are multiple error with 403 �AccessDenied� for activity
     Secret Get for Caller ID: ed87ba3a-c9f6-41c5-9c67-3040a2613f17   
 In order to get rid of this error we have to grant permission
     to the CallerID (ServicePrincipalName) From our findings there is a Service Principal that is trying to
connect to the Azure Key Vault, Microsoft.Azure.CertificateRegistration, and
its failing due to a permission issue.For that matter we tried
to add the SP to the ACLs of the Key Vault, however we could not perform the
normal operations due to the fact that there was no visibility over the
Directory information, you cannot read the directories objects due to a lock
that was set on athe resource which only premits read access.PS C:\>Set-AzureRmKeyVaultAccessPolicy -VaultName "non-prod-krispay-kv" -ObjectID ed87ba3a-c9f6-41c5-9c67-3040a2613f17 -PermissionsToSecrets Get,Set -PermissionToCertificate
Get,create,update -BypassObjectIDValidation 
 You can find this event logs under KeyVault Activity logs
     "cannot perform write operation because following scope(s) are locked
     | Please remove the lock and try again." Operation name
Update Key Vault
Time stamp
Mon May 06 2019 20:26:45 GMT+0530 (India Standard Time)
Event initiated byTest@contoso.com
Error code
ScopeLocked
Message
The scope
'/subscriptions/9048e167-320c-4204-8671-f4bf4557b1d5/resourceGroups/DEVUAT-CORE-RG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/non-prod-krispay-kv'
cannot perform write operation because following scope(s) are locked:
'/subscriptions/9048e167-320c-4204-8671-f4bf4557b1d5/resourceGroups/DEVUAT-CORE-RG/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/non-prod-krispay-kv'.
Please remove the lock and try again.
 Now how we have to remove the lock (we can remove the lock
     using below PowerShell or we can remove it from Portal under
     Keyvault>setting>Lock) PS
C:\>Remove-AzResourceLock -LockName"ContosoSiteLock" -ResourceName "/subscriptions/00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/Default-Storage-SouthCentralUS/providers/Microsoft.ClassicStorage/storageAccounts/mystorageaccount/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/locks/test" Remove-AzResourceLock  
 Ran the below command once again and this time it worked and
     now customer was able to see the new certificate. PS C:\>Set-AzureRmKeyVaultAccessPolicy -VaultName
"non-prod-krispay-kv" -ObjectID
ed87ba3a-c9f6-41c5-9c67-3040a2613f17 -PermissionsToSecrets Get,Set -PermissionToCertificate
Get,create,update -BypassObjectIDValidation Set-AzureRmKeyVaultAccessPolicy

symptom:
As of today, there are different portals that both Admins and
Users can access in a seamless way but which are developed by different teams
inside Microsoft and consequently have different implementations the user
photo. So, it�s important to understand that Microsoft online services are
composed by many different systems and workloads that may or may not be
connected with respect to the thumbnailPhoto and can show different behaviors
depending ok a) where is it reading the photo from; b) if there�s caching
involved and; c)  what�s the User license assigned.As mentioned before, there�s many portals involved in this
testing but I can only be authoritative for the Azure Portal and Account
portal. For any further questions or concerns about Office 365 portal you need
to escalate to the Office/Exchange Online team. Regarding the thumbnailPhoto attribute flow, the picture in
local AD has some limitations as it can only hold a low resolution image but
assuming the value in correctly stored in AD, AAD Connect then synchronizes the
photo into the thumbnailPhoto attribute in Azure AD. If the user does not have
an EXO license, the synchronization stops there. But when the user has an EXO
license assigned, then the thumbnailPhoto in Azure AD is copied to the
respective User�s Mailbox. This means that there�s 2 separate repositories of
the user photo in the cloud � the Azure AD thumbnailPhoto  and the
User Mailbox. These 2 repositories can get �out-of-sync� when the photo is
updated directly in the Office 365 Admin/User portal (or Set-UserPhoto) because
this process will store an HD picture in the user�s mailbox and from this point
on will
not accept any more updates coming
from the low resolution Azure AD thumbnailPhoto. In order to explain the different intricacies of the user photo
I used a simple lab with AAD Connect to test the synchronization of the
thumbnailPhoto from local AD and test the impact of updating the photo again
after it was synchronized for the first time. It didn�t require any special
tool or access rights, so this can also be tested by Support or Customer in
case there�s any doubts. These were the portals tested: 
 Account Portal (Azure AD only)
     - https://account.activedirectory.windowsazure.com/r#/applications
 Azure Portal (Cache delay) - https://portal.azure.com/#blade/Microsoft_AAD_IAM/UsersManagementMenuBlade/AllUsers
 O365 Admin Portal (Azure AD and
     User Mailbox) - https://admin.microsoft.com/Adminportal/Home
 O365 User Portal (Cache
     delay)- https://outlook.office365.com/owa/ , https://nam.delve.office.com/ , OneNote and other Office 365 workloads� Here�s a summary of the results for each portal: 
 Account Portal is the simplest
     implementation. It always reads the user photo from Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto and doesn�t have any caching - This portal will always show
     the most updated image that is stored in Azure AD thumbnailPhoto, i.e.
     that is synchronized from on-premises AD with AAD Connect. 
 Azure Portal, on the other
     hand, is the most complex. Please follow notes below from the PG � This
     portal will show the picture from the User Mailbox or the Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto for non-licensed users but there�s a caching delay. From my
     testing it can take up to 6 hours to display the updated photo.The images are
retrieved from multiple sources in the Microsoft_AAD_IAM extension in the azure
portal. Most blades retrieve them from a redis cache for performance reasons.
On those blades, depending upon how many times the cache is hit, the image may
not refresh for quite a while. This cache path also has had some issues with
certain images that causes them to be thrown out from the cache. That explains
why some images never show. We are aware of these issues, but are currently not
resourced to resolve it, because very few people notice, and it does not block
any primary scenarios.Some blades should show
the updated image immediately (the groups area) because they bypass the older
redis implementation and fetch the images directly from msgraph. This direct
fetch is the method we plan to use going forward, but there is no telling when
older blades will switch over to it. 
 The O365 Admin Portal will read
     the photo from the User Mailbox or Azure AD thumbnailPhoto if the user
     does not have a Mailbox (i.e. no EXO license assigned) � This portal will
     show the most updated photo (either from the mailbox or from the
     thumbnailPhoto) with no delays because there�s no caching involved. 
 The O365 User Portal(s) will
     always read the photo from the User Mailbox. If the user doesn�t have a
     Mailbox then it should show a blank picture since there�s no place to
     retrieve the photo, unless there�s a specific workload capable of reading
     the Azure AD thumbnailPhoto, similarly to O365 Admin Portal. � This
     portal(s) will show the picture from the User Mailbox with a delay since
     there�s also caching involved.  Conclusions With the exception of the Account Portal (which can only ready
Azure AD thumbnailPhoto) all these portals should show the same picture as long
as the user has a mailbox, but you might have to wait a few hours until you can
see the updated photo.If the Account Portal is not a concern, the best method to keep
user�s photo in sync is to discard AD thumbnailPhoto. Just make sure the user
has a Mailbox (i.e. EXO license assigned) and use the O365 Portal or
Set-UserPhoto to upload the HD image into the mailbox. In this case, allow some
time (+6hours) to see the updated picture in Azure Portal due to the cache
delay as well as other Office 365 portals.To guarantee the user photo is the same on all portals,
including Account Portal, then when a user gets its HD picture updated through
Office Portal or Set-UserPhoto, you need to also update your local AD
thumbnailPhoto with the same picture and let Azure AD Connect synchronize it to
Azure AD thumbnailPhoto.Troubleshooting Tips 
 Have the user go to Account
     Portal. This is a good check if you just want to confirm if the photo was
     correctly updated from on-premises AD to Azure AD thumbnailPhoto with AAD
     Connect.
 Use AD Photo Edit tool in case
     you�re having issues synchronizing to Azure AD thumbnailPhoto. Using the
     AD Photo Edit feature "Resize
     to recommended dimensions� guarantees that the picture is not
     exceeding AD thumbnailPhoto limitations
 After updating Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto you can immediately check in Office 365 Admin. If the
     updated picture is shown, then it was copied to the User�s mailbox, if not
     then the User Mailbox already contains an HD picture and is not accepting
     low resolution images from Azure AD thumbnailPhoto.
 After updating Azure AD
     thumbnailPhoto, wait at least 6 hours to see the updated photo shown in
     Office 365 User Portal or in Azure Portal
 After using Set-UserPhoto or
     Office 365 User Portal to update the HD photo in the User�s mailbox, wait
     at least 6 hours to see the updated photo shown in Office 365 User Portal
     or in Azure Portal

symptom:
Abstract

This article explains a common issue that is by design when
User Writeback is still enabled

The User Writeback is a feature that allows customers to
create and manage users in the cloud, similarly to cloud-only accounts, that
are then synchronized back to on-premises AD. This feature however never
reached GA and some customers enabled this feature while still in Preview.
Since this feature as been disabled on the client side � i.e. AAD Connect Sync
client � its very unlikely to still find customers with this feature enabled.

However, although User Writeback preview feature was disabled
and removed from the Wizard back in Azure AD Connect August 2015 release, this
feature persists as enabled in the Azure AD tenant DirSyncFeatures property for
customers that enabled it during Preview. If you find a customer that still has
User Writeback enabled � either on the client side or tenant side - then you
should ask to disable this feature as explained in this article.

NOTE: please make sure you advise the Customer to not enable
this feature in any circumstances since it�s not supported and it can cause AD
data loss.

 

Symptoms

Customer I trying to softmatch
a user based on UPN or Proxy Address but ADSync engine always throws the error:

Unable to update this object because the
following attributes associated with this object have values that may already
be associated with another object in your local directory services:
[ProxyAddresses SMTP:user@contoso.com;UserPrincipalName user@contoso.com;].  Correct or remove the duplicate values in
your local directory.  Please refer to
http://support.microsoft.com/kb/2647098 for more information on identifying
objects with duplicate attribute values.

 

Even if you try to hardmatch
the user by stamping the same ImmutableID in the cloud-only account, you still
get the same error.
rootCause:
This happens when UserWriteback feature is still enabled on
the customer�s Azure AD tenant. When this feature is present,
DirSyncWebServices will not process any soft-match/hard-match logic. Since users
are not �matched� together by DirSyncWebServices logic, the sync engine shows the
uniqueness exception error above. This is By
Design.

A customer can check if the tenant has this feature enabled on
the tenant by running the following command:

 

Get-MsolDirSyncFeatures | select DirSyncFeature,Enabled Support can also check this feature in ViewPoint details or
by looking in DirSyncFeatures tenant property using MSODS Explorer (GME):

 

DirSyncFeatures (1):
EnableSoftMatchOnUpn, UserWriteback,
SynchronizeUpnForManagedUsers [10272]
solution:
Firstly, we need to disable UserWriteback feature using AAD
Connect Wizard and then we can
go ahead and convert any existent �UserWriteback-users� in the cloud to normal cloud-only users from the back-end. New versions of AAD Connect will
automatically disable UserWriteback feature on the client side. 

WARNING: If this feature is no longer enabled on the client
then it�s safe to disable UserWriteback on the tenant too, but if it�s still
enabled we strongly recommend to backup any AD data associated with any
existent �UserWriteback-users� because this process may delete these objects
from AD!

 You can check if there�s any existent �UserWriteback-users�
in the cloud by running a query in MSODS Explorer (GME), as follow:Open the tenant in MSODS ExplorerGo to Objects
tabRun the query: (any) | HasOnPremisesShadow | == |
True







4.      
Any objects returned with HasOnPremisesShadow=True
are �UserWriteback-users� that may or may not have a corresponding user in AD.
This may help the customer identify which AD users were created using this
feature.

 Here�s what you need to do in order to check if User Writeback is enabled on the
client side:Run
     C:\Program Files\Microsoft Azure Active Directory
     Connect>AzureADConnect.exe /ShowUserWritebackGo
     through the Wizard until you reach the page for Optional FeaturesAt
     this page you should be able to see if User Writeback is enabled or
     disabled and then close the wizardIf this feature is still enabled on AAD Connect Wizard,
please update to the latest AAD Connect version which will safely disable the feature.
WARNING: Unchecking the User writeback option and finishing the
configuration on the Wizard will delete any �UserWriteback-users� from AD! If there�s
any �UserWriteback-users� still present in AD and User Writeback is still enabled
after AAD Connect upgrade, please escalate you support case.After confirming that User Writeback is already disabled on
AAD Connect Wizard then we can go ahead and disable it on the cloud, which will
also convert any AAD users with HasOnPremisesShadow=True and fix the soft-match
issue. 







Please raise an escalation to 'Customer Escalations - AAD Connect' to have User Writeback feature disabled from the back-end and fix-up any �UserWriteback-users�

symptom:
Didier Gautier and Thomas Vuylsteke have extended the ADFS Health Monitoring and Analytics Premium (HMA-P) offering to troubleshoot ADFS account lockouts CSS may use the ADFS HMA-P SKU in the context of a related support case The link to the entry
for this offering in the Phoenix catalog is hereThe link to the
repository on corp is here. Do not under any circumstances publish related scripts outside of Microsoft. The September 5th, 2017 version of the script is available here (chose save or open from the context menu) Installation instructions Acquire the latest version of Powerbi from https://powerbi.microsoft.com/en-us/desktop/Copy the zip file from here and extract it to c:\ Both the script and the pibx must be located in C:\HMA-P as there is no way to pass the path to the Excel report to Power BI via a parameter. The path is hardcoded in the pibx to c:\hma-P\reports.Rename get-ADFSLockoutStats.ps_ to get-ADFSLockoutStats.ps1The head of ADFSLockoutStat.ps1 contains a list of prerequisites If you don't have an ADFS environment to test the collection option, you can still test the analysis on your own machine if you have Excel and Power BI installed by running in a Powershell command prompt:              cd c:\hma-p              .\get-ADFSLockoutStats.ps1 -analyze -computername didmbr1,didmbr4(didmbr4 has no data collected on purpose). You can also directly take a look at the htlm and Excel reports in subfolder "Reports" as well as the Power BI views by double clicking get-ADFSLockoutStats.pibx from the explorer. Then click on the different elements of the views to see all graphs automatically updating. Scripts and tools use the following folder hierarchy:C:\HMA-P Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1: Script Get-ADFSLockoutStat.pbix: Pibx for Power BI C:\HMA-P\Data: Collected CSVs and CollectionInfo.xml C:\HMA-P\Logs: Log file "Get-ADFSLockoutStat.log" C:\HMA-P\Reports: Html and Excel reportsGet-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1
Expands on the script written by Ming Chen referenced in internal KB 4015604 EXOADFSAL: PowerShell script parses ADFS security event logs to identify caller IPs + targeted UPN names That version of Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1 is available for download from scriptcenter @ https://gallery.technet.microsoft.com/scriptcenter/Event-411-reader-Export-a6cd78daEnhancements in the  ADFS Health Monitoring and Analytics Premium version of Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1  includeAccepts the name of the
     computers to collect and analyze.
Specifically, you can specify the names of the ADFS servers to collect 411 event logs from without the need to save the .evtx files to a directory to then parse the events of interest. The 
     "Collect" and/or "Analyze" have been separated to allow collecting data
     from one machine and later analyzing them on another. Example syntax: .\Get-ADFSLockoutStat.ps1 -computername memberserver1,serverserver2 -collect -analyzeIt collects the following information:The account lockout     policy of the domain that the servers belong to. The audit policy and     ADFS auditing necessary to get events 411 on the serversThe security event log     of the servers. Analyzes collected data to create 3 reportsGet-ADFSLockoutStat.htm: General report about collected information and failed logon statistics. It allows checking if the audit policy is OK for generating event 411 (the audit policy of the domain, the audit policy of the members themselves and the ADFS settings regarding audit of the members). It also displays the password policy of the domain which allows detecting "risky" settings that could lead to repeated account lockouts. It then indicates the number of bad logon attempts found per servers and the top ten accounts that have experienced bad logon attempts. This report provides just an overview since detailed information are provided in the other reports. Get-ADFSLockoutStat.htm is automatically displayed once an analysis is completed. Sample output is shown belowGET_ADFSLockoutStatus.XLSXDetailed report of failed logon attempts. This is the report created by Ming Chen's "Event 411 Reader". Excel must be installed locally to get it. GET_ADFSLockoutStatus.XLSX contains the following workbooks:The list of events ID 411 collected on the servers.The list of failed logon attempts per account name and source IP address.The list of failed logon attempts per source IP address and account name.The list of failed logon attempts per source IP address, account name and time.The list of failed logon attempts per account name, source IP address and time.Sample Output Get-ADFSLockoutStat.log Contains advanced parameters with the following arguments: MaxExport: Maximum number of events to export per each evtx. 5000 events are exported by default.MaxImport: Maximum number of events to import into excel. 5000 events are imported by default.StartTime: Earliest 411 events to export.MaxThreads: Maximum number of concurrent jobs that can be run in parallel. Defaults to 4.

symptom:
AbstractThe
following Azure AD Connect versions have 2 issues with the Change user sign-in task:1.1.557.01.1.558.01.1.561.01.1.614.0Both issues impact Pass-through Authentication customers who
don�t want to use Password Hash
Synchronization. Both issues were fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0.Overview

The issue occurs in the following two scenarios:Issue 1: Password Synchronization is enabled when User sign-in method is
set to Pass-through Authentication

Scenario
1: You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where both Password Synchronization and
Pass-through Authentication are disabled.
After which, you enable Pass-through Authentication using the Change user sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you choose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, you choose Pass-through Authentication option.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization is
automatically enabled.Scenario
2: You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where Password
Synchronization is disabled, and
Pass-through Authentication is enabled.
After which, you toggle the Seamless
Single Sign-on using the Change user
sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you choose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, the Pass-through Authentication option is
already configured.You check/uncheck the Enable single sign-on option.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization is automatically
enabled.

















The background on this issue is as follows:

Prior to Azure AD Connect version 1.1.557.0,
Password Synchronization was a pre-requisite for enabling Pass-through
Authentication. Therefore, Password Synchronization was enabled when
Pass-through Authentication was enabled.Subsequently, Password Synchronization was no
longer required for Pass-through Authentication. As part of Azure AD Connect
version 1.1.557.0, a change was introduced to Azure AD Connect to remove the
enablement of Password Synchronization when Pass-through Authentication is
enabled during Azure AD Connect installation.However, the same change was not applied to the Change user sign-in task. If you use
the task to enable Pass-through Authentication to an existing Azure AD Connect deployment,
Password Synchronization is automatically enabled. This issue is fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0 to ensure that Password Hash Synchronization
remains disabled when Pass-through Authentication is enabled using the Change user sign-in task.





Issue 2: Incorrect prompt about disablement of Password Synchronization
when User sign-in method is set to Pass-through Authentication

Scenario:
You have an existing Azure AD Connect deployment where Password Synchronization
is enabled, and Pass-through
Authentication is disabled. After
which, you enable Pass-through
Authentication using the Change user
sign-in task:In the Azure AD Connect wizard, you chose the Change user sign-in task.On the User sign-in page, you chose Pass-through Authentication option.Before applying the change, the wizard
incorrectly displays a prompt that that Password Hash Synchronization will be disabled.On completion of the wizard, Password Hash Synchronization remains
enabled.









The background on this issue is as follows:

By design, if Password Hash Synchronization is
enabled, changing the user sign-in task to any other option does not disable
Password Hash Synchronization. The issue is fixed in Azure
AD Connect version 1.1.644.0 to ensure that the prompt about the
disablement of Password Hash Synchronization is not displayed.
solution:
Follow these steps to mitigate this issue on your tenant:Run Azure AD Connect and click View current configuration. Check if Password synchronization is enabled on your tenant.Disable the Password
synchronization feature:Run Azure AD Connect and click Configure.Choose the Customize
synchronization options task.On the Optional
features page, uncheck the Password
synchronization feature.Complete the wizard.Optionally, if
you want to clear password hashes already synchronized to Azure AD, follow
these steps:Ensure that the Password writeback feature is disabled
on your tenant:Run Azure AD Connect and click Configure.Choose the Customize
synchronization options task.On the Optional
features page, uncheck the Password writeback
feature.Complete the wizard.Use the Set-MsolUserPassword cmdlet to set
random passwords on all impacted users. You need to run this cmdlet 5 times for
each user because Azure AD stores the password hash history on each user (last
4 password hashes).Feedback or questions?

If you have any feedback or questions, email us at aadopauthfeedback@microsoft.com.

symptom:
Abstract

This article describes an issue where device objects that
are synchronized into the on-premises AD DS from Azure AD, using Device Writeback
in AAD Connect, may get deleted due to inactivity.  

 

Symptoms

Conditional access policies to applications and services
that require device authentication fail.
rootCause:
If device authentication is required, the user must have
registered their device and the device object synchronized into the local AD
DS.  The device is associated with the
device object and ADFS verifies this when evaluating the access policies.  If the device object doesn�t exist, the
evaluation will fail.

 

The device registration service will periodically check all
device objects in the directory and clean them up due it inactivity.  This is done to reduce AD DS bloat.  However, since the object is mastered in AAD,
the objects won�t get updated and DRS will end up deleting valid objects.
solution:
The workaround to this problem is to disable device clean-up
in ADFS. In AD FS 2016 this is set on the properties
dialog for the Device Registration Service in the ADFS admin MMC snap-in as shown below.Customers in ADFS 2012/R2 need to use PowerShell to disable this
device clean-up using the following command:  



Set-AdfsDeviceRegistration -MaximumInactiveDays 0 Note: Setting MaximumInactiveDays to value 0 disables the cleanup task.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#69315

team BEFORE following any destructive actions.

 

Abstract

This article
describes a specific issue with AAD Connect installation that may occur when
previous installations of AAD Connect / AD Sync / DirSync were installed before
on the same server and now you�re trying to re-install the latest version of AAD

Symptom A � product

that the server has currently installed. Such activity can be traced by executing

C:\Windows\Installer\MSI1D9A.tmp, Entrypoint: DetectStoreServerAction start 15:52:17: DetectStoreServer.MSI (s) (C0!08) [15:52:17:605]: Product: Microsoft Azure AD Connect
synchronization services -- Error 25019.The Microsoft Azure AD Connect
synchronization services setup wizard cannot open registry key
SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ADSync\Parameters. Try verifying the key and
running this wizard again. The system cannot find the file specified.  





































Similarly, as
in the previous symptom A, the MSI upgrading process fails because the ADsync
service entries in the registry are not present.
rootCause:
Unknown. Most
probable cause(s) are related with Windows Installer/MSI issues and not necessarily
caused by AAD Connect product itself.
solution:
In order to
fix this, we need to clean-up the inconsistency on the windows MSI database � product
code {7c4397b7-9008-4c23-8cda-3b3b8faf4312}
� referencing the Azure AD Sync Engine.
Please follow the resolution steps below in the same order as they appear.NOTE: This product code GUID values can vary depending on what is the inconsistent or stale Azure AD Sync Engine version.



ACTION PLAN #1 � Fix Windows Installer issues (if applicable)

 

There�s a
bad Windows hotfix KB3139923 that can cause Windows Installer issues. You can
check for the existence of KB3139923 under Control Panel or by using the
following steps:

                                                                             


1.            Export a list of all installed
hotfixes and look for �KB3139923� on that list: Get-Hotfix | Select HotFixID,InstalledOn,Description,InstalledBy  | Sort-Object �Descending
�Property InstalledOn
| Out-File �FilePath ".\$env:COMPUTERNAME-HotFixes.txt"   2.            If present, uninstall hotfix
KB3139923 and reboot the server.3.            Then, download and install
KB3072630, reboot again











 

ACTION PLAN
#2 � Use MSIEXEC to uninstall product code 

 

1.            Open a Command Prompt (not
PowerShell)2.            Repeat the following command a
couple of times in a row and then reboot. The MSI may encounter a ton of errors
due to the corruption.  Be sure to say
�Yes� to continue for any dialog that pops up. 
Hopefully (but not always) it will say the product code isn�t found. set /a counter+=1 & MSIEXEC /x {7c4397b7-9008-4c23-8cda-3b3b8faf4312} /qn /norestart
/l*v
"%ProgramData%\AADConnect\AADConnect_Uninstall-ForcedUninstall_a%counter%.log"
EXECUTE_UNINSTALL="1" 3.            After rebooting, I recommend
starting the wizard and immediately exiting. Then look at the log file and
determine whether you still see the stale entry warning.  If so, then move onto the action plan below.  Otherwise start the wizard again and complete
the install.













 

ACTION PLAN
#3 � Use MSIZAP � Only as a last resort

 1.            Download and install the Windows 7 SDK and select the installer tools to get the MSIZAP tool:  https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=8279 2.            Open a Command line with ''Run As Administrator" and execute this tool with the following
syntax: msizap TW! {7c4397b7-9008-4c23-8cda-3b3b8faf4312} 3.            Start AzureADConnect.exe again then
immediately exit. Check the log file again for the existence of the stale
product code.  If the problem is now gone
you should be able to start the wizard again and install without any issues.

symptom: Azure AD Connect versions 1.1.557.0 to 1.1.614.0 have issues with Change user sign-in task: (1) Password Hash Synchronization is automatically enabled when Pass-through Authentication is selected, (2) Incorrect prompt about disablement of Password Synchronization displayed when enabling PTA.
solution: Upgrade to Azure AD Connect version 1.1.644.0 or later. To mitigate: run Azure AD Connect, choose Customize synchronization options, uncheck Password synchronization on Optional features page. Optionally use Set-MsolUserPassword to set random passwords on impacted users (5 times each) to clear synced password hashes.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#69486

symptom: Azure AD Connect Health blade no longer showing up-to-date sync information. Health Insight Service crashes with Application Event 1000 for Microsoft.Identity.AadConnect.Health.AadSync.Host.exe (exception code 0xc0000374).
rootCause: Version mismatch between Azure AD Connect Synchronization Service and Azure AD Connect Health Agent for Sync. Occurs when either component was not successfully upgraded during last AAD Connect upgrade/auto-upgrade.
solution: Method 1: Manually upgrade Azure AD Connect to version 1.1.649.0 or later. Method 2: Manually reinstall Health Agent for Sync to a compatible version (3.0.127.0 or 3.0.129.0 depending on Sync Service version). After reinstall, register with Register-AzureADConnectHealthSyncAgent cmdlet.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#69958

symptom: Azure Seamless Single Sign-On not working on Windows 10 machines using MS Windows 10 Security baseline. IFrame shows Integrated Windows Authentication failed with status code 403 (Forbidden). Vanilla Windows 10 domain-joined machines work fine.
solution: Check Group Policies for Allowed Kerberos encryption type and set to Not Defined. For already affected machines: navigate to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\kerberos\parameters, delete the supportedencryptiontypes DWORD key, then reboot.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#73290

symptom: How to grant non-administrator accounts READ access to Event logs (Security, System, Application, DNS Server) on Domain Controllers and member servers (Server 2008 and above).
sourceRef: ContentIdea#73841

symptom: Support engineers delete AAD Connect Connector Space as a troubleshooting step for issues like phantom objects, join rules not working, transient objects, or objects not syncing. This is a risky operation that can cause data loss.
rootCause: Deleting Connector Space is a FIM/MIM procedure that should not be applied to AAD Connect. It can trigger massive export deletions if Sync Scheduler is still enabled, loses legitimate delete state, and prevents Product Group from investigating root causes.
solution: Run Full Import/Full Synchronization instead: Start-ADSyncSyncCycle -PolicyType Initial. If issue persists after exhausting all troubleshooting, raise an Escalation to AAD Connect Engineering team before attempting to delete the CS.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#74845

symptom: Object picker on child domain DC shows domains for which there is only a one-way incoming trust (no outgoing trust) when trying to add members to a child domain Domain Local Group. Root domain DCs are not affected.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#76726

symptom: Azure Active Directory fails to connect to SQL Database when using LocalDB. Error: SQL Server Network Interfaces: Unexpected error occurred inside a LocalDB instance API method call. Login timeout expired.
solution: Install SQL Express using a domain account to bypass using LocalDB.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#77171

symptom: After removing email address from an on-prem security group and running AAD Connect sync, the security group still shows MailEnabled=True with an initial domain (onmicrosoft.com) mail address in Azure AD.
rootCause: When an email-enabled security group is synced to AAD, an email address with the initial domain (onmicrosoft.com) is appended as secondary mail. Deleting the primary mail causes the initial domain address to become the new primary.
solution: Delete the group from Admin Portal and let it sync again, OR move the group to a non-syncing OU (filtered out) so the group is removed from cloud, then move it back.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#77896

sourceRef: ContentIdea#79362

symptom: Steps to configure Azure Active Directory to emit group claims as group names (instead of SIDs/ObjectIDs) in SAML/OIDC tokens. Requires AAD Connect custom sync rules and optional claims configuration.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#79809

symptom: Sending the custom claims in a token to the applications which can be accomplished using the custom claims mapping. Custom claims mapping is a feature in Azure AD that allows us to extend the data sent in JWT tokens and we can use it to send information such as EmployeeID, department, mobile phone in the token. Steps: 1. Install Azure AD Preview PowerShell module 2. Create a new Azure AD policy with ClaimsMappingPolicy 3. Assign the policy to a SERVICE PRINCIPAL using Add-AzureADServicePrincipalPolicy. IMPORTANT: Turn on acceptMappedClaims to TRUE in the appManifest.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#79829

symptom: Linked sign-on scenarios: Customer has many instances of application with same identifier but different relay state values, and wanted those applications to be integrated with Azure AD using SAML based single sign on. Azure AD does not allow two instances of gallery apps active with two different RelayState configurations.
solution: Use Linked single sign-on (formerly Existing single sign-on). Create another instance of app with Linked single sign on configured. Copy the user access URL from the original SAML SSO app and append it with the desired RelayState value. Assign to different users/groups.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#79830

symptom: On-premises SharePoint uses wsfed federation protocol for integration with Azure AD. Wsfed uses SAML 1.1 (Token Issuance) but Azure AD by default is configured to issue SAML 2.0 token. This mismatch causes the SharePoint Application to go in infinite loop and unable to consume the token.
rootCause: Azure AD defaults to SAML 2.0 token issuance, but wsfed/SharePoint expects SAML 1.1.
solution: Configure Azure AD to issue SAML 1.1 token for the service principal using PowerShell: Import Initialize.ps1, run Add-TokenIssuancePolicy with -SamlTokenVersion 1.1, then Set-PolicyToServicePrincipal.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#79831

symptom: Guest Users created manually in Azure AD using PowerShell (New-MsolUser with -UserType Guest) are unable to login to SharePoint site. After changing one-time password, user gets error: Something went wrong. The #EXT# string in the UPN causes the failure.
rootCause: The Usertype needs to be Member in AAD or the #EXT# string in the user name is causing the failure.
solution: Option 1: Change the member type to Member using Set-MsolUser -UserPrincipalName UPN -UserType Member. Option 2: Replace #EXT# in username with -ext.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#80035

symptom: Unable to configure Alternate Login ID using description attribute for ADFS. Customer needed an attribute other than mail as some users had UPN over 12 characters and relying applications had a 12 character limit.
rootCause: The Description Attribute is not replicated in Global Catalog PAS (Partial Attribute Set).
solution: Include description attribute to be part of PAS on the Global Catalog and allow it to be indexed for searches. Once replication happened between GCs, the command to set Alternate Login ID with Description Attribute worked.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#80036

symptom: AAD Connect AutoUpgrade stuck in Suspended state. PKS Agent acts as if Disabled (never checks for new releases). Registry key UpdateCheckEnabled is set to False (0) when it should be True (1). Affects ~80K tenants.
rootCause: Bug introduced in build 1.1.524.0 (May 2017): UpdateCheckEnabled registry key is set to False whenever AutoUpgrade state is set to Suspended. Fixed in build 1.1.750.0.
solution: Manual upgrade to build 1.1.750.0 or later. Alternatively, run the Repair-ADSyncDiagsAutoUpgradeState PowerShell script which fixes the registry key and restarts AzureADConnectHealthSyncMonitor service.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#80833

symptom: VSTS will no longer allow new MSA users with custom domain names. Azure AD tenant needs to be connected to VSTS. When trying to disconnect AAD tenant from VSTS, AAD tenant disconnection failed error occurs.
solution: Connect Azure AD to Team Services account via VSTS settings. Clear cached credentials at %userprofile%/local settings/application data/Microsoft/VisualStudio Services/7.0, then re-authenticate.

symptom: Azure new PowerShell Get-AzureADDevice does not have a deviceID operator built in. It only has Get-AzureADDevice -ObjectId, making it impossible to look up devices by deviceID directly.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#84197

symptom: Unable to access the Relying Party configured on ADFS. Error: POLICY0018: Query to attribute store Active Directory failed: The supplied credential is invalid. Error code: 49, LdapErr: DSID-0C09056D, AcceptSecurityContext error, data 569. Network trace shows BindResponse: Status: Invalid Credentials from DC.
rootCause: Missing Registry Value MSOIDSSP under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages.
solution: Add MSOIDSSP value under registry HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa\Security Packages and reboot the server.

symptom: Unable to change UPN from user@contoso.com to user@contoso.onmicrosoft.com. Error: Set-MsolUserPrincipalName: Access Denied. You do not have permissions to call this cmdlet.
rootCause: There is an existing stale object entry in Azure AD which has to be removed at ORGID side.
solution: Run GME ID Consistency checker and submit ICM to Orgid team to remove the stale object entry.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#90817

symptom: When attempting to delete an Autopilot Deployment Profile, error: Cannot delete <AutopilotProfile Name>. The profile is assigned to groups. You must unassign all groups from this profile before you can delete it.
rootCause: This occurs when: 1) An active group is still assigned to the Autopilot Deployment Profile, or 2) A group was assigned to the profile but deleted from Azure AD before removing the assignment, leaving an orphaned link that cannot be removed via portal.
solution: Remove all groups from Intune portal (Device Enrollment > Windows enrollment > Autopilot deployment profiles > Assignment). If the group was deleted from AAD, use Graph Explorer DELETE request to /beta/deviceManagement/windowsAutopilotDeploymentProfiles/<ProfileID>/assignments/<ProfileID>_<GroupID> to remove the orphaned assignment, then delete the profile.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#91658

symptom: How to publish on-prem SAML Claim Aware application using Azure AD Application Proxy. Covers configuring SAML application federated with Azure AD using AAD-AP, including pre-authentication setup and SSO configuration.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#91754

symptom: AAD Connect Synchronization Service Manager delta or full AD import operations fail with "No DirSync control data returned." Connection Log shows "Failed-Search" status. Event ID 6050 (ADSync) reports connectivity issues on the management agent.
rootCause: TCP packet fragmentation causes data integrity issues. Improper or non-matching TCP Offloading settings between Domain Controller and AAD Connect server cause PayLoadLen mismatches during packet reassembly. Outdated networking drivers may also contribute.
solution: On both Domain Controller and AAD Connect server: disable all TCP Offloading options in advanced network adapter settings, enable Receive Side Scaling (RSS), update networking adapter drivers, then reboot both servers and run a delta import.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#92329

symptom: After migrating users with ADMT and SID history from source to target domain, users cannot access file shares in the source domain. Effective permissions for the migrated user show empty on the file server, despite SID filtering being disabled between forests.
rootCause: Expected behavior of Built-in groups. The "Domain Users" built-in group membership from the source domain does not carry over via SID history for file share permission evaluation.
solution: Option 1: Create a custom group in the source domain, add the migrated user, and grant that group permissions on the share. Option 2: Add the target domain "Domain Users" group directly to the folder permissions.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#94045

symptom: SQLVDI and SQLWriter errors on servers running Azure AD Connect cause VM backup failures. VSS error: SqlServerWriter state VSS_WS_FAILED_AT_PREPARE_SNAPSHOT (0x800423f4). LocalDB errorlog shows BackupVirtualDeviceSet::Initialize CoCreateInstance failure with 0x800703fa (Illegal operation attempted on a registry key marked for deletion).
rootCause: AAD Connect uses LocalDB under AAD_xxx user context and forces user instance timeout to 65535 (preventing LocalDB shutdown). When the sync session ends, Windows unloads the user registry but LocalDB stays active. Subsequent VDI/COM calls fail because COM classes were unloaded with the orphaned registry.
solution: Workaround 1: Activate Local Policy "Do not forcefully unload the user registry at user logoff" (KB2287297, may need reboot). Workaround 2: Revert LocalDB user instance timeout to default (60 min idle shutdown). Check AAD Connect product defect #315878 for permanent fix status.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#94117

symptom: SSPR_0029 error on Password Reset Self Service Portal: "We are unable to reset your password due to an error in your on-premises configuration." Event 33004 PasswordResetService: "The password could not be updated because the management agent credentials were access denied." Event 6329 ADSync: unexpected error during password set operation.
rootCause: AAD Connect Password Writeback uses SAM RPC. A Group Policy SAM RPC hardening setting (Network access: Restrict clients allowed to make remote calls to SAM) on the DC restricts SAM connections to Admins only, and the AAD Connect service account is not a domain admin.
solution: Add the AAD Connect AD DS connector account to the allowed SAM RPC callers list. Use PowerShell: Import-Module "C:Program FilesMicrosoft Azure Active Directory ConnectAdSyncConfigAdSyncConfig.psm1"; Get-ADSyncADConnectorAccount to identify the account, then add it to the GPO security descriptor.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#95533

symptom: Customers see the following prompt on the top right corner when they are in Azure Portal UI within the Enterprise Applications.Clicking the above prompt gives them the following page.
rootCause: As the second page above indicates, this prompt happens if there is a Reply URL that starts with https://127.0.0.1:444/...This was a Reply URL that was added by MS earlier and is known to cause signin issues. Hence MS PG decided to remove it. However being on the customer's tenant, we did not want to remove it from the backend.This configuration can be verified by using ASC ( Azure Support Center) as well.
solution: 1. If the customer hasn't added the Reply URL above using the Application Registrations node, then they can do one of the following:a. In the second dialog above, there is a button at the bottom on the page to "Remove Incorrect Reply URL". Simply Clicking it will fix it.b. They can remove it manually from the Application Registrations node, ApplicationName. Properties and Reply URLs. Simply highlight the one with the above Ip 127.0.0.1 and delete it.c. Lastly they can do within the Manifest for the application in the Application Registrations. NOTE: Editing the manifest can make the application unstable and hence use proper caution to just remove the one line under Reply URL section.2. If the customer added the URL themselves manually and are experiencing signin issues, then please test removing the reply URL and confirm if the issues go away. If the customer wants to keep the URL, then please initiate a discussion in appropriate Teams channel or  open ICM for PG for comments
sourceRef: ContentIdea#97656

symptom:
Customer tries to configure the service now provisioning in the Azure Portal. On the Provisioning Page (Dashboard => Enterprise Applications - All Applications => Service Now Provisioning) the
administrator fills the required fields for provisioning and after click on Test Connection the following error occurs:

You appear to have entered invalid credentials. Pleas confirm you are
using the correct information for an administrative account.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Troubleshooting steps:


 Required Information


Following
information is required for further troubleshooting


 Tenant Name (initial domain)
 Time and Date when the issue
     occurred
 Time Zone of the machine,
     where this config was made


 

 


 Open ASC and convert the
     Initial Domain Name to tenant ID


It
is a good thing to ask the customer for the tenant ID, where the issue
occurred, because our support request might be related to another tenant of the
same customer.

  

 


 Open Kusto Explorer and run
     the following query


 

IfxUsageEvent

|
where contextId
== "06770ba4-zzzz-xxxx-yyyy-929feb7dcf78"
// this is the tenantID

|
where env_time
> ago(4d) 

|
where message
contains "validateCredentials"

|
project env_time,
correlationId, message

 

Returned
a list of recent attempts to validate credentials via the portal. 

 

 


 From the output of 4 took the
     correlationId from newest attempt, which matched your screenshot�s
     timestamp, and ran this:


 

IfxUsageEventWithCorrIdUrl

|
where correlationId
== "837cb19e-xxxx-yyyy-zzzz-86ebb31e54f2"
| project url

 

And
from that it gave me a Jarvis URL 

 

 


 Copy the url and open in in a
     browser


 and remov the default filter of Warning events

and
sorte by env_seqNum � timestamps in these logs only go down to the seconds, 

so
we�ll have a whole bunch of events at say e.g. 03-21-2019 14:22:20 and the
ordering is incorrect. 

The
env_seqNum attribute is the actual �what order did these events occur in�
counter and sorting by that makes them 100% accurate in order. 

Once
I sorted by env_seqNum, scrolling down to the end I was able to find three
things � a request out to ServiceNow, a response from ServiceNow, and an error
generated by the response:

 



  

 

 


 In this output we see
     following important messages:


 

%%pii_WEB REQUEST :: Performing an
operation on a Web resource. Resource: https://vi*****.service-now.com/sys_user.do?SOAP Operation: http://www.service-now.com/sys_user/getRecords Headers: Content Type: Data:
<getRecords xmlns="http://www.service-now.com/sys_user">
<user_name>azure.provisioning</user_name> </getRecords>

 

 

And
then they respond with: 

 

%%pii_WEB RESPONSE :: Received
response from Web resource. Resource: https://vi*****.service-now.com/sys_user.do?SOAP Operation: http://www.service-now.com/sys_user/getRecords Response:
<getRecordsResponse></getRecordsResponse> Request: WEB REQUEST
:: Performing an operation on a Web resource. Resource: https://vi*****.service-now.com/sys_user.do?SOAP Operation: http://www.service-now.com/sys_user/getRecords Headers: Content
Type: Data: <getRecords xmlns="http://www.service-now.com/sys_user">
<user_name>azure.provisioning</user_name> </getRecords>

 

All
of the stuff in the response after the highlighted yellow part is a repeat of
our request � ie: they say �here is your response, also after my response here
is your original request so you know what
I am responding to�.

 

Our request out to ServiceNow was �give me the user
azure.provisioning�s information�, and they respond with <getRecordsResponse></getRecordsResponse>, ie: a completely empty response. No error, but no
data.

 

Looking
one line down from that, we see:

 

Error code:
ServiceNowAdministratorImport Scope: RunProfile Transient? False Tenant
Actionable? True Body:
Microsoft.ActiveDirectory.Connector.UserReportableException: Although we received no
authentication or authorization error from ServiceNow, we failed to read the
user identified by your credentials. That outcome indicates that the credentials may
in fact be invalid, or
the user identified by the credentials has insufficient permissions. Please provide a current
ServiceNow administrative user name and password along with the name of a valid
ServiceNow instanceConclusion:We
should really be surfacing that error to the customer but it looks like we
aren�t. In any case, this looks like some sort of permissions issue inside of
ServiceNow. If the customer uses a SOAP explorer/request tool and sends the
same request that we sent using the same account.  The customer should look into why we
are not being given a meaningful response here, and if necessary engage
ServiceNow support to help resolve that issue.

symptom:
When Conditional Access Policy is created to force MFA
on Exchange Online (EXO) and/or SharePoint Online (SPO) cloud application, and
the users open the browser and try to create Word/Excel/PowerPoint from https://portal.office.com portal. The
configured Conditional Access Policy will prevent them with the following
error:
rootCause:
When user tries to create Word/Excel/PowerPoint document from https://portal.office.com portal, the selected application tries to access Exchange Online (EXO) and/or SharePoint Online (SPO) using non-interactive login. And since MFA does not complete with non-interactive login, the authentication will fail, and the user will get the above error. From Fiddler trace: GET: https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/oauth2/authorize?client_id=00000002-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000&redirect_uri=https%3a%2f%2foutlook.office365.com%2fowa%2f&resource=00000002-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000&response_mode=form_post&response_type=code+id_token&scope=openid&msafed=0&client-request-id=ed2367c1-edcc-4637-9a07-cd99de2deffe&protectedtoken=true&prompt=none&login_hint=user1%40zmaililab.onmicrosoft.com&nonce=636890311591198484.cf66b484-c576-4825-ba79-cf6f593e7cbf&state=pYvBisIwFEXb8VvirrFp2rRZFBHEpQwjfkAaXmigMSVJrc4Pz2_43LkfePdxuNyTZ1m2wXxh8hJf1gouOllyxhrJmOzqrqbaCDEgFLppRVF3VVMMqpUF9qaRHFo9mBzdv3znV7W7LDbBBcLdavgO_vGkKs6PffyorxHCWTnoFwRG6vLXKTvZSQ3U35zVwUdvEtXebT-1H0hLuF3D1I8pzZHwA6lOeOu6Um8MLt7Ku-MntaSR8GNFKjF6B4hsG_7pq9neIfTsBQ Response: Working...
From logs Miner: OAuth2 authorization request: { %%pii_removed%%}; client_id=00000002-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000&redirect_uri=https%3a%2f%2foutlook.office365.com%2fowa%2f&resource=00000002-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000&response_mode=form_post&response_type=code+id_token&scope=openid&msafed=0&client-request-id=ed2367c1-edcc-4637-9a07-cd99de2deffe&protectedtoken=true&prompt=none&xxxxxx=%7bEmailHidden%7d&nonce=636890311591198484.cf66b484-c576-4825-ba79-cf6f593e7cbf&xxxxxx=pYvBisIwFEXb8VvirrFp2rRZFBHEpQwjfkAaXmigMSVJrc4Pz2_43LkfePdxuNyTZ1m2wXxh8hJf1gouOllyxhrJmOzqrqbaCDEgFLppRVF3VVMMqpUF9qaRHFo9mBzdv3znV7W7LDbBBcLdavgO_vGkKs6PffyorxHCWTnoFwRG6vLXKTvZSQ3U35zVwUdvEtXebT-1H0hLuF3D1I8pzZHwA6lOeOu6Um8MLt7Ku-MntaSR8GNFKjF6B4hsG_7pq9neIfTsBQ&cxh_inclusive=False OAuth2 error response: https://outlook.office365.com/owa/ -- (error: interaction_required, error_description: AADSTS50076: Due to a configuration change made by your administrator, or because you moved to a new location, you must use multi-factor authentication to access '00000002-0000-0ff1-ce00-000000000000'. Trace ID: 79e01448-7e78-470a-aa3b-673018da1700 Correlation ID: ed2367c1-edcc-4637-9a07-cd99de2deffe Timestamp: 2019-03-24 13:32:44Z, state: pYvBisIwFEXb8VvirrFp2rRZFBHEpQwjfkAaXmigMSVJrc4Pz2_43LkfePdxuNyTZ1m2wXxh8hJf1gouOllyxhrJmOzqrqbaCDEgFLppRVF3VVMMqpUF9qaRHFo9mBzdv3znV7W7LDbBBcLdavgO_vGkKs6PffyorxHCWTnoFwRG6vLXKTvZSQ3U35zVwUdvEtXebT-1H0hLuF3D1I8pzZHwA6lOeOu6Um8MLt7Ku-MntaSR8GNFKjF6B4hsG_7pq9neIfTsBQ, )
solution:
Since this is non-interactive login, MFA
will not trigger, and the MFA popup will not show to the end user. Hence, creating
Word/Excel/PowerPoint documents from office portal will fail.

Therefore, we need to modify the configured
Conditional Access Policy to target �All Cloud Applications� instead of targeting
Exchange Online (EXO) and/or SharePoint Online (SPO).

The idea behind that, once the user open
office poral, he will be forced to provide MFA. Then when he tries to create Word/Excel/PowerPoint
documents from Office portal, MFA will be required again, but in this scenario,
MFA claim is satisfied in the browser session cookies from the first MFA at the
begging, and the user will be able to create the needed documents successfully.

 

Note: we are targeting �All
Cloud Applications� because there is no cloud application to target office
portal yet.
