symptom:
This article is still in development and is not to be shared with customers yet.
  
  
     
  
  
    Applies to customers who have enabled Conditional Access policies for iOS and macOS device platforms.
  
  
     
  
  
    For any questions on this issue, please contact Sandeep Deo 
    
      sandeo@microsoft.com
    
    .
  
  
     
  
  
    We recently discovered that Apples launch of iOS13 scheduled for mid-September will introduce a breaking change that could impact Microsoft Azure AD and Intune customers that use Conditional Access policies in their organization. This notice is intended to help you understand the breaking change from Apple and evaluate the impact to your organization, and to provide several recommendations from Microsoft.
  
  
     
  
  
    What is the breaking change?
  
  
    In iOS13, Apple is introducing a new OS, the iPadOS. All iPads that update to iOS 13+ will have their OS updated from iOS to iPadOS. While some of this new OS will behave similarly to iOS, there are key apps, such as Safari, that are identified as and operate now as macOS. Since CA policies are often applied on an OS- or App-specific basis, this change could affect your security and compliance of any iPad device that upgrades to iPadOS.
  
  
     
  
  
    What is impacted?
  
  
    Theres a set of apps used for Conditional Access that will be affected since these apps are now macOS-identified apps. In reviewing your CA policies, youll want to focus on whether you provide access through any compliance or security checks using the affected app categories. 
  
  
     
  
  
    Below are the app categories that will 
    impact 
    enforcement of your CA policies on iPad running iPadOS.
  
  
    
    Safari browser
  
  
    
    Apple Native mail
  
  
    
    Apps that 
    do not use
     Microsoft identity platform 
    
      v2.0 authentication libraries
    
     or 
    
      v1.0 authentication libraries
    
  
  
     
  
  
    There is 
    no impact
     to the below app categories.
  
  
    
    All Microsoft apps including Edge browser
  
  
    
    Chrome browser
  
  
    
    
      Apps
    
     that use 
    
      Intune App SDK
    
     or 
    
      App wrapping tool for iOS
    
  
  
    
    Apps that use Microsoft identity platform 
    
      v2.0 authentication libraries
    
     or 
    
      v1.0 authentication libraries
    
  
  
     
  
  
    Lets walk through a couple of scenarios that could be impacted before we get to Microsofts recommendations. 
  
  
    1)
    Youve setup a CA policy that requires an approved client app for email access and you have no policy configured for macOS. In this case, once a device updates to iPadOS, the 3
    
      rd
    
     party mail app will not enforce the CA policy. 
  
  
    2)
    Youve setup a CA policy that requires a compliant device in order to access company resources from an iPad. However, you did not configure a macOS compliance policy, as your executives have macs and requested exemption from all CA policies. Now, when the iPads update to iOS 13, users will be able to access corporate resources from iPads on a non-compliant device since CA is not targeted to macOS devices. 
  
  
    3)
    3  (we can put in a third example, but for now, lets see what happens at the LT level). 
  
  
     
  
  
    What is Microsofts recommendation?
  
  
    The following table illustrates the impact in detail and the recommendation from Microsoft with respect to a CA policy configuration you may have in place for your organization.
  
  
     
  
  
    
      
        
          
            Conditional Access policy configuration
          
        
        
          
            Impact
          
        
        
          
            Recommendation
          
        
      
      
        
          
            iOS
          
        
        
          
            macOS
          
        
      
      
        
          
            You have one of these configured: 
          
          
            1)
            Require approve client app/Require app protection policy
          
          
            2)
            Require approve client app/Require app protection policy
          
          
            AND
          
          
            Require compliant device
          
          
            3)
            Require approve client app/Require app protection policy
          
          
            OR
          
          
            Require compliant device
          
          
            4)
            Require compliant device
          
          
             
          
        
        
          
            No policy
          
        
        
          
            Accessing your organizations resources from apps in the impacted app category described above will result in bypassing approved client app /app protection policy/ compliant device policy and providing unfettered access.
          
        
        
          
            
            We highly recommend configuring a Conditional Access policy for macOS to require a compliant device.
          
          
            
            If this is not an option for you at this time, we recommend falling back to using Require MFA. 
          
          
            Note
            : Having no policy for macOS will provide zero security.
          
        
      
      
        
          
            Require compliant device
          
        
        
          
            Accessing your organizations resources from apps in the impacted app category described above will result in bypassing approved client app /app protection policy and asking user to enroll their device with Microsoft Intune.
          
          
            Note:
             No impact if you have #3 or #4 configured for iOS. 
          
        
        
          
            Educate users to use apps from the non-impacted app category.
          
        
      
      
        
          
            Require MFA
          
        
        
          
            Accessing your organizations resources from apps in the impacted app category described above will result in bypassing approved client app /app protection policy/ compliant device policy and prompting user for MFA.
          
        
        
          
            
            If this behavior will not work for your organization, we recommend that you configure a Conditional Access policy for macOS to require a compliant device. 
          
          
            
            If this is not an option for you at this time, we recommend you educate users to use apps from the non-impacted app category described above.
          
        
      
      
        
          
            Block Access
          
        
        
          
            Accessing your organizations resources from apps in the impacted app category described above will result in bypassing approved client app /app protection policy and blocking access.
          
        
        
          
            If this behavior will not work for your organization, we recommend that you educate users to use apps from the non-impacted app category.
          
        
      
      
        
          
            Terms of Use (consent per device)
          
        
        
          
            No Terms of Use (consent per device)
          
        
        
          
            Accessing your organizations resources from apps in the impacted app category described above will result in not prompting users for consenting to terms of use.
          
        
        
          
            Educate users to use apps from the non-impacted app category.
          
        
      
    
  
  
     
  
  
    
      
     
  
  
    Will there be additional 
    updates
    ?
  
  
    We are continuing to explore other alternatives to minimize the impact of this breaking change from Apple to our customers Conditional Access policies. We anticipate having more information to share soon, and will provide this to you. 
  
  
     
  
  
    Please feel free to reach out to Microsoft Support if you have any further questions.
  
  
     
  
  
    Thank you,
  
  
    Microsoft Azure AD Team
sourceRef: ContentIdea#107772

symptom:
AAD Connect is installed and configured in Hybrid identity scenario with a
�Multiple forest, single sync server, user represented in only one directory�
topology using the ms-DS-ConsistencyGuild as a sourceAnchor attribute.
More details:https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/hybrid/plan-connect-topologies#multiple-forests-single-sync-server-users-are-represented-in-only-one-directoryhttps://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/hybrid/plan-connect-design-concepts#using-ms-ds-consistencyguid-as-sourceanchorDue to design considerations, some customers have an organizational
structure configured in such a way where the reporting manager and direct
report are represented in two different Active Directory forests.
rootCause: The on-premises ActiveDirectory attribute �Manager� is a reference attribute and contains the distinguished name of the user who is the user's manager. The manager's user object contains a directReports property that contains references to all user objects that have their manager properties set to this distinguished name.https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/adschema/a-managerIn the Active Directory Users and Computers MMC you would not be able to set as manager a user from a different forest.The attribute "Manager" is a referential type attribute and it resolves into the DistinguishedName as you can see from the ldifde output. That is why, you can only set DNs from the same forest.LDIFDE output of test user:Dn: CN=User ForestA,OU=ADUsersForestA,DC=amanet,DC=localaccountExpires: 9223372036854775807 (never);badPasswordTime: 0 (never);badPwdCount: 0;cn: User ForestA;codePage: 0;countryCode: 0;displayName: User ForestA;distinguishedName: CN=User ForestA,OU=ADUsersForestA,DC=amanet,DC=local;dSCorePropagationData: 0x0 = (  );givenName: User;instanceType: 0x4 = ( WRITE );lastLogoff: 0 (never);lastLogon: 0 (never);logonCount: 0;manager: CN=Manager ForestA,OU=ADUsersForestA,DC=amanet,DC=local;mS-DS-ConsistencyGuid: <ldp: Binary blob 16 bytes>;name: User ForestA;objectCategory: CN=Person,CN=Schema,CN=Configuration,DC=amanet,DC=local;objectClass (4): top; person; organizationalPerson; user;objectGUID: a450ea49-2ff8-4e00-a6cd-923a13045108;objectSid: S-1-5-21-2553677299-1332292137-3163259927-277102;primaryGroupID: 513 = ( GROUP_RID_USERS );pwdLastSet: 8/28/2019 2:26:11 PM Coordinated Universal Time;sAMAccountName: user.forest1;sAMAccountType: 805306368 = ( NORMAL_USER_ACCOUNT );sn: ForestA;userAccountControl: 0x10200 = ( NORMAL_ACCOUNT | DONT_EXPIRE_PASSWD );userPrincipalName: user.forest1@amanet.local;uSNChanged: 2335377;uSNCreated: 2331811;whenChanged: 8/29/2019 10:49:59 AM Coordinated Universal Time;whenCreated: 8/28/2019 2:26:11 PM Coordinated Universal Time;If the manager�s user is not present in the forest where the employee�s user is located, you will not be able to resolve the DN.This behavior is by design.
solution:
As a workaround to bypass this scenario, you can try to
leverage the Azure AD Connect synchronization engine to manipulate the metaverse objects and achieve your goal. Depending on the requirements of the customer, there
are two possible scenarios:Option 1: Assign using a contact objectYou can create in forest A that will correspond to the
manager�s user account that is in forest B, the two object types will be merged
by Azure AD Connect based on the email address attribute. Metaverse
view of the managers user account after merged with contactThe contact can be added as a manager reference to the original
user account. Make sure that you select the Contacts object type to be able to retrieve
the contact.Assignment
viewAfter running the synchronization and inspecting the MV
object for the user account, the manager reference will point to the Manager
user account:                                                                   Checking the Azure Active Directory identity for the
user you will find the same type of reference, pointing to the manages Azure
Active Directory object.Option 2: Assign using a join it into the
metaverse with a new disabled account created in forest B.By design in the current topology, user object types
from two distinct connectors are joined in the metaverse after they are
processes by the In from AD � User Join synchronization rule and the
joining criteria is that the mS-DS-ConsistencyGuid is identical across the two
accounts. For this scenario to work, you will need to create in
forest B a new disabled account for the �employee�.For the object in forest B to be merged with the object
in forest A, they need to share the same value for the ms-DS-Consistency-Guid
attribute (sourceAnchor).Metaverse
object view of the user account after mergingYou can then assign the manager in forest B as a
reference on the disabled user account that is linked to the active account in
forest A (3-rd connector)Metaverse
object view of active user account

symptom:
What is this attribute and how is it used? The process that drives populating and using
this attribute is part of the Next Generation Credentials Technology (commonly
known as � NGC). More
about NGC and Microsoft passport: TechCommunity: Microsoft Passport and Azure AD: Eliminating passwords one device at a time!   There are multiple services and devices that are
capable of NGC. Windows hello for business is just one of our services that
uses the password less authentication flow. For your reference, in
the following article you have an example attached on how the Windows hello for
business flow works and how the value for msDS-KeyCredentialLink is
processed. Learn: Hybrid Azure AD joined provisioning in a key trust deployment in a managed environment   The msDS-KeyCredentialLink user
attribute in Active Directory stores the asymmetric key (public part) needed
for the user account identity in the authentication flow. The key stored under
the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute will be used to first identify the ser
on-premise and then authenticate him. In case of Windows Hello for Business the
asymmetric key written under msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute needs to be
available both on the user and machine object, as it is directly related to
both.  The searchableDeviceKey attribute associated to the user object Azure
Active Directory is populated once the user has a linked device that can
perform NGC (there are numerous types of devices & applications that can
use NGC and you can have linked to your  Azure Active Directory account, for
e.g. authenticator app, USB drive for fingerprint scan, biometrics scan etc.). How is this attribute populated and synchronized?Once a device is linked to the Azure AD user
and searchableDeviceKey calculation is completed, the following flow is being
followed by the Azure Active Directory Connect tool to writeback msDS-KeyCredentialLink.
The value is first being read from Azure AD nd provisioned to the AADC
metaverse by the following rule: New-ADSyncRule  ` -Name 'In from AAD - User NGCKey' `-Identifier '53d9ba9f-f519-443f-ad24-b5344a757XXX' `-Description '' `-Direction 'Inbound' `-Precedence X `-PrecedenceAfter '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' `-PrecedenceBefore '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' `-SourceObjectType 'user' `-TargetObjectType 'person' `-Connector 'b891884f-051e-4a83-95af-2544101c9XXX' `-LinkType 'Join' `-SoftDeleteExpiryInterval 0 `-ImmutableTag 'Microsoft.InfromAADUserNGCKey.001' `-OutVariable syncRule Add-ADSyncAttributeFlowMapping  `-SynchronizationRule $syncRule[0] `-Destination 'deviceKey' `-FlowType 'Expression' `-ValueMergeType 'Update' `-Expression 'IIF(IsNull([searchableDeviceKey]), NULL, IIF(CNum(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],1,1))=0, Mid([searchableDeviceKey],1,7+CNum(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],5,2))) & CBin(Len(Thumbprint(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],8+CNum(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],5,2)),Len([searchableDeviceKey])))),2) & CBin(2,1) & Thumbprint(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],8+CNum(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],5,2)),Len([searchableDeviceKey]))) & Mid([searchableDeviceKey],8+CNum(Mid([searchableDeviceKey],5,2)),Len([searchableDeviceKey])), NULL))' `-OutVariable syncRule In short, the algorithm from the ADSyncAttributeFlowMappingExpression evaluates the searchableDeviceKey attribute from the user
present in Azure Active Directory and checks if the value is compliant. If this
is met, we will update the deviceKey attribute (present in the
Metaverse) with the value of the searchableDeviceKey attribute from
Azure Active Directory.    SearchableDeviceKey
attribute value present in MSODS:   {<###iceKey ###ge="#" ###Identifier="#/###IVKGN6dfEighnuKrHv+###aBti4MtpGwnO8GFQ=" ###ationTime="###9-##-###19:##:##.###4674Z" ###iceId="###69004-###a-###4-###c-###ccc85d700" ###tomKeyInformation="###AAQACAAAAAAAAAAAA" ###ns="###p://###emas.###rosoft.###/###ine/###ectoryservices/###nge/###8/##"><###Material>###BMQAIAAADAAAAAAEAAAAAAAAAAAAAAQAB4blIn9hQoiBrx5GjTrUrlZhf99TcOnydK9Ttmf5crA4ab3MLEunVcE69ubI2OYjZMU9btdRPGfgB4xbqvWZlWxxNFv6PgRo7ZlS8rEa2Draef8t4VMxR7Dw/###C6YFl1htjrZo0qOOuaAEj3g2QpFmF/###C4eExvYnbXgDt52QgXqB6kEIY7gJHHVGrFDgHWQsfZzDuBPuelEp377exMt5KZBcocz6gmyxnOha10hOEynQFlZO9idv2vKiIhUcd4QqsRXR8vtoGyd5ANHTaK2H+###JmmA4I0CxTiOD8qnfGXFuoVlcojv7O+###GF4MEQGiHkxmJSd5l22t1QEQ==</###Material></###iceKey>}  Next in the synchronization flow, the deviceKey
attribute will be exported from the Metaverse to the local Active Directory
connector space, processed by the following rule:   New-ADSyncRule  `-Name 'Out to AD - User NGCKey' `-Identifier '515d25ba-4ae7-4622-b67a-6f3811f8eXXX' `-Description '' `-Direction 'Outbound' `-Precedence X `-PrecedenceAfter '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' `-PrecedenceBefore '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000' `-SourceObjectType 'person' `-TargetObjectType 'user' `-Connector '58b27e0e-e269-488a-8cdf-32899a06cXXX' `-LinkType 'Join' `-SoftDeleteExpiryInterval 0 `-ImmutableTag 'Microsoft.OuttoADUserNGCKey.001' `-OutVariable syncRuleAdd-ADSyncAttributeFlowMapping  `-SynchronizationRule $syncRule[0] `-Source @('deviceKey') `-Destination 'msDS-KeyCredentialLink' `-FlowType 'Direct' `-ValueMergeType 'Update' `-OutVariable syncRuleNew-Object  `-TypeName 'Microsoft.IdentityManagement.PowerShell.ObjectModel.ScopeCondition' `-ArgumentList 'sourceAnchor','','ISNOTNULL' `
               -OutVariable condition0     This basically translates in: If the scoping
condition for this rule is met (user is synchronized so sourceAnchor is
populated), the value of the deviceKey will be updated to the msDS-KeyCredentialLink
attribute. This attribute is further-on exported to the user object present in
Active Directory, if the Active Directory Connector account has the right
permissions on ADDS.

rootCause:
For PIM service
to be able to access Azure resources, MS-PIM SPN should always have a
User Access Administrator role assigned on a subscription.In this case the User
Access Administrator role for PIM SPN (MS�PIM) was accidently removed from the

listed RBAC roles was missing for PIM service on a subscription.

subscription level and that should allow PIM service to access the Azure
resources.Note: The
role can be assigned on a management group or subscription level based on the
requirements and set-up of the customer�s tenant.

symptom: When customers review their sign in Logs, they notice that the User IDs show up as User: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000Username: domainName.comUser ID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000Alternate sign-in name: Application: ApplicationXYZApplication ID: Resource: ResourceNameXYZResource ID:  Client app:  The above kind of logs can have both success and failure as their Status.This in-turn affects their log analysis logic, where they try to find out malicious sign ins, by filtering out the sign in logs with UPNs that contain domain names other than the ones that are already present as verified in their domain, or the domain names that they have guest users from. This also raises concern to the customers, since they do not find who the user is, and they suspect malicious activity in their tenants.
rootCause: This happens due to setup of Tenant restrictions.Tenant restrictions on a network, is basically setup using a proxy device that inserts headers into traffic to Azure AD endpoints. The two headers that are inserted are  :Restrict-Access-To-Tenants:  (use a value of <permitted tenant list>, which is a comma-separated list of tenants you want to allow users to access.)Restrict-Access-Context: (use a value of a single directory ID, declaring which tenant is setting the tenant restrictions.)For more on how this is setup, please refer : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/manage-apps/tenant-restrictions#configurationLet us consider an infrastructure of Tenant A, where they have configured their internet proxy device in a way that inserts headers in their network, as follows :Restrict-Access-To-Tenants: TenantA.onmicrosoft.com, TenantB.onmicrosoft.com, TenantC.onmicrosoft.comRestrict-Access-Context: DirectoryIDofTenantALet us consider the following scenarios now :1) User from tenant A, accesses an application from tenant A internally (making use of Tenant A's Infrastructure's network, where the headers are inserted by the proxy devices that exist in the infrastructure, as per the tenant restrictions feature)  In this scenario, a Sign-In log will be generated with all the details, in two places :               -- Sign in Logs of Tenant A               -- Azure Active Directory blade > Overview > Other Capabilities > Tenant Restrictions.2) User from tenant B, accesses an application from tenant B, from a different network than tenant A's network ( basically external to tenant A's network )In this scenario, a sign-in log will be generated with all the details in                 -- Sign in logs of Tenant B3) User from tenant B, accesses an application from tenant B, from tenant A's network where the proxy devices have been configured to insert headers in the traffic to Azure AD, as per the tenant restrictions feature mentioned above.In this scenario, the sign in logs will be as follows :                 -- Sign in log containing all information in tenant B's sign ins , as usual.                 -- Sign in log containing partial information in tenant A's Sign in logs.                 -- Sign in log containing partial information in tenant A's Azure Active Directory blade > Overview > Other Capabilities > Tenant Restrictions.The partial information will show up in the format :User: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000Username: domainName.comUser ID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000Alternate sign-in name: Application: ApplicationXYZApplication ID: Resource: ResourceNameXYZResource ID:  Client app:  where domainName.com is the domain of the user who is trying to login. ( In our scenario, this will be TenantB's domain name )
solution: The customer will have to take this Tenant Restrictions' sign in logs into considerations when they run scripts to analyze the logs.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#113635

symptom: Users/Customers claim that they cannot see their Line-Of-Business applications on Azure AD's Conditional Access Application picker, when creating new Conditional Access policies.
rootCause: This can happen when the user has created the application as a native app, or does not have a Web App platform configured under its Platform Configurations in Authentication sections of the application's app registration portal.Conditional Access does not apply to native apps , because it wouldn't provide any real security. A caller can still just always call the API directly.Hence, the user needs to protect the APIs that the native application is trying to access, with Conditional Access, rather than protecting the Native Application itself.For info on what is a native application : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/native-app
solution: The resolution on this is to apply Conditional Access to the available APIs that the application is invoking/accessing, or to the service that the API falls under.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#113769

symptom: While joining a Linux VM in a step we need to run kinit <User's UPN> this step may throw the below errors,kinit: Cannot find KDC for realm '<Domain FQDN>' while getting initial credentialskinit: Resource temporarily unavailable while getting initial credentialsIn this article we will see why the error occurs and how to troubleshoot and resolve them.What is kinit?kinit is a command in Linux operating system which is used to obtain and cache an initial TGT(Ticket Granting Ticket) from the DC.
rootCause: kinit: Cannot find KDC for realm '<Domain FQDN>' while getting initial credentialsNo definition for the domain FQDN in krb5.conf file.kinit: Resource temporarily unavailable while getting initial credentialsThe nameserver records are missing in resolv.conf file.
solution:
1) kinit: Cannot find KDC for realm '<Domain FQDN>' while getting initial credentials
 This
     error occurs if there is no definition in the /etc/krb5.conf file for the
     kdc of domain you are trying to join the machine to.(Usually the domain is
     picked from the UPN suffix).
 If
     this error occurs, please follow the below steps to add the entry in krb5
     file.
 Run
     the command vi /etc/krb5.conf press "i" to start write mode in
     vim editor.
 Add
     the entry in the below syntax after the [realms] tag in the file,<Domain
FQDN in capital letters> = {kdc =
<dc fqdn or IP Address>kdc =
<dc2 fqdn or IP Address>}
 Save
     and close the file by pressing escape key and wq followed by an enter.
 Now
     try running kinit.2) kinit: Resource temporarily unavailable while getting initial credentials1.     
This error occurs
because the nameserver record is missing in the /etc/resolv.conf file2.     
Open the
resolv.conf file using the command vi /etc/resolv.conf3.     
Add the entry as
below,nameserver
<IP of DC>nameserver
<IP of DC2>4.     
Save and close the file by pressing escape key
and wq followed by an enter.5.     
Now try running kinit.Note:If user's UPN suffix and the created domain name is different use <user samAccountname>@<Domain FQDN> this will join the machine to domain because Linux actually thinks the UPN suffix of the user is the domain and searches for the UPN suffix as the Domain. For example, If the user domain is "a.local" and username is "human@a.com" while joining to the domain use "human@a.local" this will search for the user "human" in "a.local" so, this will succeed and the machine will join to the domain.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#114254

symptom: When customers try to customize error messages on ADFS page they see duplicate entries of the attributes as:The above ADFSGlobalWebContent has duplicate attributes but with different values for some and similar values for some other attributes.Secondly when the error messages are customized the customer still sees the same old error message displayed on the ADFS error page.
rootCause: The reason being that each locale has its own web content and customization of the error pages. When the customization is done without specifying the locale it by default updates the Global web content where the value of locale is "NULL".In order to customize the error messages for a particular locale you need to explicitly call out the name of the locale while running the PowerShell cmdlet as below: To avoid confusion you can remove the locale which is not required in environment by below command: Remove-AdfsGlobalWebContent -Locale en
solution: In order to customize the error messages for a particular locale you need to explicitly call out the name of the locale while running the PowerShell cmdlet as: Set-AdfsGlobalWebContent -Locale  en -ErrorPageGenericErrorMessage "<Message>"
sourceRef: ContentIdea#114391

symptom:
This article describes how to unjoin a Windows 10 computer that has been Microsoft Entra joined to a Microsoft Entra tenant.  OOBE Setup in Windows 10 Enterprise presents the user with two "domain join" options to choose from.  
   Join Azure AD   Choose this option if your organization uses O365 or other business services from Microsoft. Your organization might collect information about you, install or remove apps, change settings or disable features, delete content, or reset your device. Talk to your support staff to learn more.  
  
   
  
  Join a domain   If you plan to join your PC to a domain, we'll help you set up a local account now. When setup is finished, join the domain as you have in the past. 
  
   
   The first option allows you to join the device to Microsoft Entra.  This allows a user to enter a UPN logon name in the form of JoeUser@contoso.com. If Device Registration is enabled on the Microsoft Entra tenant, then the device can join.  Microsoft Entra join injects the user's SID from Microsoft Entra ID into the local Administrators group of the computer. Currently, Microsoft Entra join does not create a computer object in the on-premises Active Directory.  The second option is to legacy domain join to an Active Directory domain. Choosing the second option has you create a local account on the computer first. Once you are logged on with the local administrator account you can join the computer to a domain.  The user selected the first option during OOBE and performed a Microsoft Entra join because the Microsoft Entra tenant had Device Registration enabled.  The user logged on successfully but discovered they cannot access all of their domain resources.  When the user checks System Properties in Control Panel they discover the device is still in a WORKGROUP.  When the user attempts to join the domain, it fails with the following popup:       Screenshot  Title: Computer Name/Domain Changes   Message Body:  The following error occurred attempting to join the domain "corp.microsoft.com":  This machine is already joined to a cloud domain and cannot be subsequently joined to an Active Directory domain.    Examining the NetSetup.log under %systemroot%\debug shows:   07/27/2015 09:16:52:068 -----------------------------------------------------------------07/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin07/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin: using current computer names07/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin: NetpGetComputerNameEx(NetBios) returned 0x007/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin: NetpGetComputerNameEx(DnsHostName) returned 0x007/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin: DJ request on a CDJ-ed device, failing07/27/2015 09:16:52:068 NetpDoDomainJoin: status: 0xa8c  Where error code 0xa8c translates to:  Hex  Decimal  Symbolic  Friendly / Extended   0xa8c  2700  NERR_AlreadyCloudDomainJoined  This machine is already joined to a cloud domain and cannot be subsequently joined to an Active Directory domain    1. Open Local Users and Groups mmc snap-in (lusrmgr.msc) using Run as an Administrator 2. Create a New user account and make it a member of the Administrators group, then verify login with this account works.  3. Open an Administrator command prompt and run these commands:    - The first command should show a Device State of AzureAdJoined : YES    - The second command should delete the local next generation credential for the current user. If present, copy the Device ID that appears in the "Unjoin message".        dsregcmd.exe /status    dsregcmd /leave /verbose /debug    4. Have a global administrator login to the Devices blade of the Microsoft Entra ID portal and delete the Device ID copied from the "Unjoin message".  NOTE: If Microsoft Intune is used, delete the device from Intune's Devices blade as well.  5. Reboot the computer and log in using the local Administrator account you used to unjoin Microsoft Entra.  6. Once you are logged in you can rename the Workgroup name, or you can join the device to an Active Directory domain using System Properties in Control Panel.  You can also join it to the Active Directory domain from the Settings app under System \ Accounts \ Access work or school by clicking Connect and entering the UPN of your work account and selecting Join this device to Azure Active Directory.

The below steps can help collect the appropriate logs from the Kubernentes cluster when troubleshooting Azure AD Login issues with AKS.Open the terminal and connect to cluster.Run az aks get credentials without the admin flag (With

     set is for the cluster local admin).Run
     export

symptom: As per article https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/active-directory-saml-claims-customization#adding-application-specific-claimsI want to add new attribute name for example "OrganizationID" with a static value="Contoso1234" I cannot enter Contoso1234 at Source Attribute. It wont save.I have a requirement to add the new attribute with static value. How can I add an attribute with static value?1. In the Azure portal, on the User Attributes & Claims section, click on the Edit icon to edit the claims.2. Click on the required claim which you want to modify.3. Enter the constant value without quotes in the Source attribute as per your organization and click Save.4. The constant value will be displayed as below.
rootCause: "Source Attribute" field would not let you type anything static. The alphabets will keep on deleting as you type them.
solution: Type "Contoso1234" in Notepad instead of typing it directly under "Source Attribute" tab and copy / paste it there. Click on "Contoso 1234" as highlighted and you will not see it after selecting but you can click on SAVE and you are good to go.

symptom: Enable the debug logs by following this article https://supportability.visualstudio.com/AzureAD/_wiki/wikis/AzureAD/183983/Azure-AD-Sign-in-for-Azure-Linux-VMs?anchor=auth.log-(debug-level)in the log you will see the below error,vmTextMining sshd[48164]: pam_aad(sshd:auth): Cannot find the cloud instance for location CentralIndiaThis issue is caused because the metadata sends the location name as IndiaCentral and Plugin is searching for CentralIndia.
rootCause: This issue is caused because the metadata sends the location name as IndiaCentral and Plugin is searching for CentralIndia.
solution: This issue is fixed in the Plugin release 1.0.011360001 to install this plugin please follow the below steps,Install the extension with the below command,az vm extension set \--publisher Microsoft.Azure.ActiveDirectory.LinuxSSH \--name AADLoginForLinux \--resource-group <rg name> \--vm-name <vm name>Login to the VM with a local root account.Create a directory under home directory named plugin using the command mkdir plugin.Navigate to plugin directory using cd plugin.Run the command for Ubuntu version 18.04 curl -O https://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/18.04/prod/pool/main/a/aadlogin/aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.deb for Ubuntu version 16.04 use curl -O https://packages.microsoft.com/ubuntu/16.04/prod/pool/main/a/aadlogin/aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.debRun the command apt remove aadlogin.Once the previous command completes run the command dpkg -i aadlogin_1.0.011360001_amd64.debNow if you run the command apt show aadlogin the version will be 1.0.011360001This should update the Plugin set to the recent version and if the install is successful you will get a response as shown in the screenshot below,Restart the sshd service.Now when you try login the devicelogin flow will be triggered.
sourceRef: ContentIdea#114923

symptom: Taking Examples : App name : <Application Name> with App ID : 2fddb45a-3701-403d-8063-69c4f8531838Attribute value ( Role ) : Service-based_Development_OperationsAttribute name in analysis : displayNameUnique Identifier for the role: idCustomer's Application that is setup for provisioning, when it goes to quarantine, gives the following logs (or you figure this out using the Kusto Queries) :Here is the current reason for the quarantine: EncounteredQuarantineException.The code for the recent error code was DuplicateAppRoles.Here is the most recent error message: "This Azure Active Directory service principal has app roles with duplicate attribute values: 2fddb45a-3701-403d-8063-69c4f8531838: "<Application Name>".  The attribute with the duplicate values is displayName.  The duplicated value is Add:"Service-based_Development_Operations" (Source).  The synchronization job cannot proceed until the duplication is remedied.  One way of remedying the duplication would be to edit the service principal using the Azure Active Directory Graph or the Microsoft Graph.  Both of those Web application programming interfaces are documented on the World Wide Web.  If the documentation is insufficient, please file a request for support using the Microsoft Azure Active Directory Graph or the Microsoft Graph via Azure support.
rootCause: Usually when this happens, the cause is that the role that currently exists in the application with that name is not the same role that previously had it. Each role has a unique identifier -  "id" in this case. During the initial times of the application�s creation and usage, AAD would have imported the available roles into the application object for usage, out of which, "Service-based_Development_Operations" is one of them Our AAD service sees the current role in <Application Name> and sees that no role exists in AAD with the ID value of 67890 ( example number used for the role name Service_Based_Development_Operations ) . It then tries to create the role, but encounters a duplicate attribute conflict as there is an existing role with the name Service-based_Development_Operations already (which was imported initially), but it has a different anchor/ID attribute value 12345 ( Example number again that is essentially different from the current value of ID for the same role in <Application Name> ) . At the current moment, our service does not resolve such mismatches by itself.Hence the new incoming role is conflicting with the role that we currently have for this application in Azure AD ( Conflict with display name being same for existing role with a given ID, and an incoming role with a new ID )The solution here would be to delete the role for the serviceprincipal object through Graph. Once that is done, the incoming role should not have any problems in getting created on our side and hence, would have you avoid the current situation of duplicate Roles . ( You will need to use a PATCH command to update the approle for the service principal through graph, where the body will contain the updated app roles list. To get the current list for the service principal you can run a GET command on graph as well for the service principal object)PS: Huge thanks to Danny Zollner who helped me understand the cause!
solution: 1) First, take a look in ASC using the AppId in the right tenant, to see if this role ( you can search with the id  ) is present as part of the serviceprincipal's roles, or the application's roles definition. ASC > Azure AD Explorer > Applications > AppId > Service Principal / ApplicationObject2) Making a note of the object ID of the service principal / application object where you noticed the AppRole under investigation was presentRun the following in graph Explorer ( Sign in first there ) :GET https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/serviceprincipals/02c5872b-a48e-4892-802d-86ecda0fed5e Format : https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/serviceprincipals/ObjectIDofServicePrincipal  Get the approles definition from here.For instance : "appRoles": [        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "User",            "displayName": "User",            "id": "4fe59fae-f463-4713-857a-ae9e82a35498",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        },        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "msiam_access",            "displayName": "msiam_access",            "id": "0931ab05-cb33-4fb9-a3ea-c56f9d06f312",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        },        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "Test for user/group",            "displayName": "User/group role",            "id": "4fe59fae-f463-4713-857a-ae9e82a35628",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": "grouptest"        }    ] -- The highlighted role for instance is the one that was being complained about, and is the one we need to remove, so that we can import the new role --3) Edit this to first disable the role in question. PATCH https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/serviceprincipals/02c5872b-a48e-4892-802d-86ecda0fed5eformat : PATCH sameURL body:  (note the additional { and } to make this a json formatted body )New app role definition like below :{    "appRoles": [        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "User",            "displayName": "User",            "id": "4fe59fae-f463-4713-857a-ae9e82a35498",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        },        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "msiam_access",            "displayName": "msiam_access",            "id": "0931ab05-cb33-4fb9-a3ea-c56f9d06f312",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        },        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "Test for user/group",            "displayName": "User/group role",            "id": "4fe59fae-f463-4713-857a-ae9e82a35628",            "isEnabled": false,            "origin": "Application",            "value": "grouptest"        }    ] } 4) Now, to remove the role mentioned, keeping the formatting in mind when you do that. ( Commas, and the paranthesis ) [ In the example above, delete the entire highlighted part, along with the comma above that part to have the new app role definition ready.Once that is done, change the mode to PatchPATCH https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/serviceprincipals/02c5872b-a48e-4892-802d-86ecda0fed5eformat : PATCH sameURL body:New app role definition like below :{    "appRoles": [        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "User",            "displayName": "User",            "id": "4fe59fae-f463-4713-857a-ae9e82a35498",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        },        {            "allowedMemberTypes": [                "User"            ],            "description": "msiam_access",            "displayName": "msiam_access",            "id": "0931ab05-cb33-4fb9-a3ea-c56f9d06f312",            "isEnabled": true,            "origin": "Application",            "value": null        }        ] }This should remove the app role from the Service principal's definition.Post this, the provisioning should succeed when you restart.

symptom: Seamless SSO doesn't work as expected. Users get prompted for Password during sign in.
rootCause: After we hit login.microsoftonline.com, Azure AD detects that the tenant is enabled for seamless SSO and redirects the user as below. User gets Unauthorized challenged from Autologon Endpoint.  User fetches Kerberos ticket 1:48:14 PM x/xx/xxxx             3528        36.4694666                             xx.xx.xx.xx            xx.xx.xx.xx               KerberosV5             KerberosV5:TGS Request Realm: domain.com Sname: HTTP/autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com           {TCP:193, IPv4:190}1:48:14 PM x/xx/xxxx             3530        36.4754347                             xx.xx.xx.xx            xx.xx.xx.xx               KerberosV5             KerberosV5:TGS Response Cname: test_user   {TCP:193, IPv4:190} Kerberos ticket is presented back to Azure AD  Azure AD responds with 403 Forbidden error.   This is because Azure AD refused to accept the ticket that was presented by the user.
solution: Searched for the correlationID in the response in Kusto to get further details.//Get all PerRequestTableIfx events associated with the CorrelationId cited in the client-side error to get a Summary viewlet start = datetime(xxxx-xx-xx 08:26:00Z);let end = datetime(xxxx-xx-xx08:28:00Z);find in (cluster("estsam2").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estsbl2").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estsby1").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estsch1").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estsdb3").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estshkn").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estssin").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estsdm1").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx,cluster("estssn1").database("ESTS").PerRequestTableIfx) where CorrelationId == "8523xx-xxxx-xx-xx-2dd374fa6409"//| project env_time, CorrelationId, RequestId, Result, ApplicationId, ErrorCode, MdmAppId, IsDeviceCompliantAndManaged, DeviceTrustType, MfaStatus, ClientIp, ClientIpSubnet, DeviceId, ApplicationDisplayName, ConditionalAccessAppIdentifier1, ConditionalAccessAppId1Decision, ConditionalAccessAppIdentifier2, ConditionalAccessAppId2Decision, MultiCAEvaluationLog, ConditionalAccessVerboseData, ClientInfo, Client //Remove for more detail| sort by env_time ascWhen the difference between client timestamp and the server is greater than the configured value(5 mins default), �KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW: Clock skew too great" error occurs. We adjusted the timesync issues between client machine, DC and global time and issue got resolved.

symptom: We have created ADFS claim rule to block all external request except the particular Active directory group of users. After creating the rule, we still see connections are allowed and the requirement was not satisfied. ADFS is not issuing Deny claims when users in AD group "S-1-5-21-1010517290-1075059173-903097961-179968" comes from external network. Below is the rule we created:exists([Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/2012/01/requestcontext/claims/x-ms-proxy"])&& exists([Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/claims/groupsid", Value =~ "S-1-5-21-1010517290-1075059173-903097961-179968"])&& NOT exists([Type == "http://schemas.microsoft.com/2012/01/requestcontext/claims/x-ms-forwarded-client-ip", Value =~ "\b1\.1\.1\.([1-9]|[1-9][0-9]|1[0-9][0-9]|2[0-5][0-9])\b"])=> issue(Type = "http://schemas.microsoft.com/authorization/claims/deny", Value = "true");The claim rule here is set to issue deny if the user�s authentication comes through ADFS Proxy (WAP) and if the user belong to Group with SID - S-1-5-21-1010517290-1075059173-903097961-179968 and also if the user�s authentication has not originated from the x-ms-forwarded-client-ip location mentioned (which is a trusted IP address)We confirmed the user is a part of the group in Active directory.
rootCause: In ADFS Audit / debug logs, when claim processing was happening, the group was not being evaluated. The group SID S-1-5-21-1010517290-1075059173-903097961-179968 assigned in the ADFS claim rule was for a distribution group and not a Security group.
solution: Changed the group type to Security group and ADFS started processing the group claims and issued deny claim as it should.

symptom: Azure AD joined machines take approximately 3 hours  (=2.77 hours) to boot up the logon screen. The machine is not hung and you will see a black screen with spinning dots. 2.77 hours later, after the boot is complete, interactive logon works correctly.The problem happens when the following conditions are met.1. AzureAD is synced on-premises AD.2. Client's Work Group name and Domain NetBIOS name of the on-premises are same.
rootCause: When you logon with AzureAD account synced with on-premises AD, the domain information of the on-premises AD is set to the client OS. If the on-premises Domain NetBIOS name and the Work Group name of the client are the same, the deadlock occurs after the rebooting client.
solution: Change the Work Group name of the client to a different name than the Domain NetBIOS name of the on-premises AD. This bug will be fixed in the future release.

symptom: ADFS gives a responder token back to relying party and the event viewer logs the below eventLog Name:      AD FS/AdminSource:        AD FSDate:          6/23/2020 10:13:38 AMEvent ID:      316Task Category: NoneLevel:         ErrorKeywords:      AD FSUser:          ****************Computer:      *****************************Description:An error occurred during an attempt to build the certificate chain for the relying party trust ********************* certificate identified by thumbprint '<Cert thumbprint>'. Possible causes are that the certificate has been revoked, the certificate chain could not be verified as specified by the relying party trust's signing certificate revocation settings or certificate is not within its validity period.You can use Windows PowerShell commands for AD FS to configure the revocation settings for the relying party signing certificate.Relying party trust's signing certificate revocation settings: CheckChainExcludeRootThe following errors occurred while building the certificate chain:  A certificate chain could not be built to a trusted root authority.The revocation function was unable to check revocation for the certificate.The revocation function was unable to check revocation because the revocation server was offline.
rootCause: This mostly happens when the relying party sends a signed request to ADFS server and ADFS server could not verify the revocation of the certificate which is used to sign the authentication request.
solution: First thing we need to do is to identify the certificate from the thumbprint found in the event 316 in ADFS admin log. Once done export the certificate to file path.Then run the below command to verify if the revocation is getting successful or not.certutil -f �urlfetch -verify "<Path of the exported RP signing cert>"In case the revocation fails you will get an error as below The above error means the CRL distribution list is not accessible from the ADFS server. The CRL distribution list can be checked from the cert properties as below Resolution: There are three options to resolve the issueMake the CRL distribution list accessible from the ADFS server (Most recommended)Force the ADFS relying party trust not to perform the revocation check of the certificate. Please run the below command to set the revocation check to NoneGet-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -Identifier <Identifier URL> | Set-AdfsRelyingPartyTrust -SigningCertificateRevocationCheck NoneAsk the application not to sign the authentication request. (Least recommended)

     domain/forest and single AAD Connect server.

symptom: Azure AADConnect service fails to start with an error 'Error 1069: The service did not start due to a logon failure'
rootCause: This happens when the Adsync service cannot use the credentials associated with the VSA (Virtual Service Account) of 'NT SERVICE\ADSYNC'
solution: 1. Please confirm through ASC that the service account used for Azure AADConnect is 'NT Service\ADSYNC'.  2. Using Service.MSC please Navigate to ADSync service with the Display Name as 'Microsoft Azure AD Sync'3. Please click on the Logon tab and then please specify the logon account as 'NT Service\ADSYNC'.4. Please clear the password fields if it is already populated with a value or otherwise please do not specify any value in Password and confirm password fields.

symptom: When trying to start the ADFS / AD FS service within the services console, it fails with the following error: The Active Directory Federation Services service terminated with the following error: An exception occurred in the service when handling the control request.  The AD FS Admin Event log (within Event Viewer) shows the following errors:  ====================== Log Name:      AD FS/AdminSource:        AD FS  Date:          12/7/2022 3:47:39 PM  Event ID:      381  Task Category: None  Level:         Error  Keywords:      AD FS  User:          CONTOSO\adfssvc  Computer:      adfs.contoso.local  Description:  An error occurred during an attempt to build the certificate chain for configuration certificate identified by thumbprint '0ED1F75D0503DD8D0D148946D010A032193C5'. Possible causes are that the certificate has been revoked or certificate is not within its validity period.   The following errors occurred while building the certificate chain:    MSIS2013: A required certificate is not within its validity period when verifying against the current system clock. ======================  ====================== Log Name:      AD FS/AdminSource:        AD FS  Date:          12/7/2022 3:47:39 PM  Event ID:      102  Task Category: None  Level:         Error  Keywords:      AD FS  User:          CONTOSO\adfssvc  Computer:      adfs.contoso.local  Description:  There was an error in enabling endpoints of Federation Service. Fix configuration errors using PowerShell cmdlets and restart the Federation Service.  Additional Data   Exception details:   System.ArgumentNullException: Value cannot be null.  Parameter name: certificate     at System.IdentityModel.Tokens.X509SecurityToken..ctor(X509Certificate2 certificate, String id, Boolean clone, Boolean disposable)     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.Service.Configuration.MSISSecurityTokenServiceConfiguration.Create(Boolean forSaml, Boolean forPassive)     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.Service.Policy.PolicyServer.Service.ProxyPolicyServiceHost.ConfigureWIF()     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.Service.SecurityTokenService.MSISConfigurableServiceHost.Configure()     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.Service.Policy.PolicyServer.Service.ProxyPolicyServiceHost.Create()     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.ServiceHost.STSService.StartProxyPolicyStoreService(ServiceHostManager serviceHostManager)     at Microsoft.IdentityServer.ServiceHost.STSService.OnStartInternal(Boolean requestAdditionalTime)   User Action:   Ensure that the certificate is valid and has not been revoked or expired. ======================    Trying to update the ADFS certificates fails with this error:  Update-AdfsCertificate : Could not connect to net.tcp://localhost:1500/policy. The connection attempt lasted for a time span of 00:00:02.0046596. TCP error code 10061: No connection could be made because the target machine actively refused it 127.0.0.1:1500.
rootCause: This can occur if the ADFS Token Signing and Token Decrypting certificates have expired, and the system was not able to roll over to new certificates. This situation may be most common when using a VM template, or if a VM was shut down during the certificate expiration timeframe.
solution: The ADFS service won't start because the certificates are expired, and you can't update the certificates unless the ADFS service is started.  You have to trick the system into thinking the certificates are valid long enough to get the service started, then you can update the certs.  We can do that with PowerShell:  # Set the system date back to before the certificates expired, and then start the service.   # Run these together so that service starts before the system time automatically changes back. set-date -date "10/01/2022 06:11" start-service adfssrv  # Update the two expired certificates Update-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType "Token-Signing" -Urgent Update-AdfsCertificate -CertificateType "Token-Decrypting" -Urgent

symptom:
The MDM Terms of Use are set in the Mobility (MDM and MAM)
blade of Azure Active Directory for the MDM applications defined here. These
include Intune, 3rd party On-Premises MDM Applications, and 3rd
party cloud MDM�s.   The MDM User Scope set inside of the MDM application will
determine who is targeted by the Terms of Use.   As seen above, a User scope of �All� would mean all AAD
users would receive the MDM policy containing the configured Terms of Use for
this �On Premises MDM Application�. If you target �Some�, you will be able to
set a group for this MDM. To verify which users are a part of group, check Azure Support Center (ASC) or run a graph query. See List
group members - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn for more details. You will also want to
ensure that if a user is assigned an MDM policy, they are licensed with a valid
Azure Active Directory Premium license. If they are using Intune, an active
Intune license will also be needed. This can be checked with Rave, ASC, or
Graph once again. Lastly, ensure that you do not have overlapping User scopes
for separate MDM�s. If a user falls under more than one MDM, the MDM they are
directed to will be indeterminant.  Getting
started with Azure Active Directory Premium | Microsoft Learn Licenses
available for Microsoft Intune | Microsoft Learn List
licenseDetails - Microsoft Graph v1.0 | Microsoft Learn    The MDM applications configured in the Mobility blade along
with their user targeting create a backend policy in Azure known as the Default MDM Policy.  We can verify this
by checking with Assist365 and Graph. To check in Assist365, go to 'Applications > Intune >
Troubleshooting > Policy > AAD Policies'. There you should see a policy
named �DefaultMDMPolicy�. This will include the corresponding Application ID�s
of the MDM�s in the Mobility section of Azure along with their user scopes.    You can verify the AppID�s listed here by using
ASC. �0000000a-0000-0000-c000-000000000000� is the
AppID for Intune in every tenant.  To check with Graph, run this query: GET https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/mobileDeviceManagementPolicies  Graph will return an individual Mobile Device Management Policy
for each MDM configured in the customer�s tenant.    If you are noticing more policies returned than what is configured
in Mobility for Azure AD, then you will want to trace down the AppID�s listed.
If the ID�s do not return anything, you will want to delete these ghost MDM
applications as they can prevent the correct MDM policy from being applied to
devices. That can be done with this Graph command: DELETE https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/policies/mobileDeviceManagementPolicies/"APPID" When a user goes to register/join a device with Azure and have an
MDM properly set with the terms configured and scope, Windows should prompt for
MDM enrollment. The flow takes place on steps A-D below:     An ID token issued to the user during device registration
should contain claims that represent the Terms and Enrollment URL.  Cloud
Domain Join Web App sends a GET request to discover Auth endpoints returning
the configurations (login.microsoft.com/commonopen-id). With
the auth endpoints retrieved, it builds a sign in request *GET Call) to obtain
an ID_Token to Azure Device Registration Service. This token should contain the
URL�s as seen above.         At this point, the device should be redirected to the Terms
and Service for your MDM. The terms for a 3rd party MDM are hosted
independently and will redirect to the URL set regardless of validity.
Typically, if there is an issue here, the �Microsoft-Windows-Shell-Core/Operational�
would be the best log to investigate. If the Terms of Use never pop up during the enrollment flow
and you have verified everything in the article so far, do the
following:  1. Run DSREGCMD /Status with the UPN that performed the Azure enrollment. You should see the Terms with their account. Make sure you run this in a normal
user context. If the device is only Azure AD registered, it is normal for there
not to be a PRT present.                2. If
the terms are listed here, we may need to investigate the ��Microsoft-Windows-Shell-Core/Operational�
event log to see if there was an issue with the Cloud Experience Host in
Windows. If
network traffic is clean, we will want to perform an authentication trace
during an Azure enrollment scenario where the endpoint should display the
terms.        Troubleshoot
hybrid Azure Active Directory-joined devices - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft
Learn Look
at the NCG.ETL to see what the token for the user returned. If it returns null
values, we know there is an issue with the MDM policy set by Azure as the user
ID token is not receiving the proper claims. You
could also check this by decoding the TBRES files for the user in %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\TokenBroker\Cache      The screenshot above shows an instance where the claims for the
MDM values are not found on the token. At this point, we would want to send the
case to the AAD Auth team to further troubleshoot as the Azure Evo-STS is the
owning product team and it may require an ICM.

symptom:
You are using AAD Joined Windows Scenario the
appliance of Manage account protection settings
with endpoint security policies in Microsoft Intune | Microsoft Learn. In this policy you have configured to remove(update) the default
Memberships of "Authenticated Users" and "Interactive"
users with the SID values of these categories. In the same policy you have
chosen to add(Update) a AAD group. The issue experienced is that although the
values are being delivered and applied, the evaluation of the AAD group membership
is not working
rootCause:
AAD Group membership evaluation currently does not
work for the built-in group of "Users"
solution:
Two proposed workarounds.  1st Solution    create a new local group add the SID of the AAD
     Group to this newly created local group on the device grant "Allow log on locally" to the local group in order for the members of the group to
     have the necessary permissions to log on to the device remove the local
     "Users" group in order for other users part of the same tenant
     to not have access to the device   The
steps presented above are the manual steps that you can use in order to arrive
to the desired scenario that you have presented to us.   Since
some of the steps presented in the workaround are not available for
configuration, this solution presented will not be completely configurable
through the Intune Service.    2nd Solution (as this solution can be applied remotely, it has been chosen as the main
alternative)      As
such a second workaround that will provide you with a working environment would
be to create a "Settings Catalog policy for User Rights" where you define the
"Administrators" group and all the individual cloud users that you
require access to the device. This workaround will allow you to define the
users that have log on access to the device, and the users list can be modified
from the Intune Portal. All other default members of the "Allow log on
locally" are going to disappear as they are not going to be configured in
the policy, and as such the permissions will be restricted only to
administrators and the users you have configured individually. In a way
the policy itself will act as the group of users that will have access to the
device. Example: AzureAD\example@contoso.com

collab task for an Azure type application, such as CloudSync.  The customer gets an error in the
wizard/application stating, "The specified directory service attribute or

provides a vague statement similar to the following: [14:43:38.350]
[ 18] [ERROR] ConfigSyncDirectoriesPage: Caught exception while creating the
connector for directory: domain.contoso.com. Exception
Data (Raw): System.Management.Automation.CmdletInvocationException: The
specified directory service attribute or value does not exist.  --->
System.DirectoryServices.ActiveDirectory.ActiveDirectoryOperationException: The
specified directory service attribute or value does not exist.  --->
System.Runtime.InteropServices.COMException: The
specified directory service attribute or value does not exist.      at
System.DirectoryServices.DirectoryEntry.Bind(Boolean throwIfFail)    at
System.DirectoryServices.DirectoryEntry.Bind()    at
System.DirectoryServices.DirectoryEntry.get_AdsObject()    at
System.DirectoryServices.DirectorySearcher.FindAll(Boolean findMoreThanOne)    at

---    at
System.DirectoryServices.ActiveDirectory.Forest.GetDomains()    at
System.DirectoryServices.ActiveDirectory.Forest.get_Domains()   Directory Services
is requested to troubleshoot this error, or even get an iDNA/tttracer of this
application.
rootCause:
Directory
Services is not responsible for troubleshooting this error.  It is the application team's responsibility
to not only troubleshoot this error, but to improve their logging to improve serviceability as well as allowing the customer to self-resolve the issue.  Requesting DS to gather an iDNA of this
application is the least preferred and least efficient method. DS will assist if application team actionable information such as permissions, missing objects, etc.
solution:
If collab owner, do
not proceed without an ICM created by Azure application's team for
troubleshooting this error.  ICMs are
used for escalation, and should be used here to gain awareness of this scenario. 
 If case owner, do
not allow Azure application's team to resolve the collaboration until they have
provided the object they are running into issues with and the nature of the failures.   If the case is a
CritSit, you can attempt to use LDAP client
tracing to guess what the application is doing. 
However, this
does not absolve the application owner of their responsibilities.   From  https://supportability.visualstudio.com/WindowsDirectoryServices/_wiki/wikis/WindowsDirectoryServices/418762/LDAP-Configuration-and-interoperability   LDAP Client Tracing
Instructions: Start tracing: Reg add
HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\ldap\Tracing\&ProcessName.exe&
/f logman create trace
"ds_ds" -ow -o c:\ds_ds.etl -p
"Microsoft-Windows-LDAP-Client" 0x1a59afa3 0xff -nb 16 16 -bs 1024
-mode Circular -f bincirc -max 4096 -ets   Stop tracing: logman stop
"ds_ds" -ets Reg delete
HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\ldap\Tracing\&ProcessName.exe&
/f   So, for CloudSync
install, it would look like this: Start tracing: Reg add
HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\ldap\Tracing\AADConnectProvisioningAgentSetup.exe
/f logman create trace
"ds_ds" -ow -o c:\ds_ds.etl -p
"Microsoft-Windows-LDAP-Client" 0x1a59afa3 0xff -nb 16 16 -bs 1024
-mode Circular -f bincirc -max 4096 -ets   Stop tracing: logman stop
"ds_ds" -ets Reg delete
HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\ldap\Tracing\AADConnectProvisioningAgentSetup.exe
/f     Example of guesswork
triage for a CritSit 5836
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6199746 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] ScopeOfSearch=0, SearchFilter=(objectClass=*),
DistinguishedName=CN=Computers,DC=contoso,DC=com, AttributeList=objectClass,
ProcessId=0x1E10 5894
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6205234 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=ldap_search returned 0x0 for
connection 0x713748d8: DN was
CN=Computers,DC=contoso,DC=com. SearchScope was 0x0. 5895
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6205273 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=HrStartReadSequence ran out of data, length 0x40, offset 0x40. 5896
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6205291 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=LdapGetValues 8 connection
0x713748d8 received protocol error 0x10
. 5897
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6205309 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=LdapGetValues connection
0x713748d8 did not find attribute 'o' 5898
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6205332 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=ldap_get_values had decoding
error of 0x10 . 5899
[1]1E10.165C::04/05/23-12:17:07.6215784 [Microsoft_Windows_LDAP_Client/Debug16
] Message=ldap_unbind called for connection 0x713748d8: host is 'something'.   This was a base
search of the computers container, and "something" went wrong.  In this case, we got lucky that this search
was relevant and quickly found that they had denied Auth Users Full Control to
